topics.title: Mesh and Node Analysis of Networks with Independent and Dependent Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mesh and Node Analysis of Networks with Independent and Dependent Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mesh Analysis (Loop Analysis / Mesh Current Method):**
A systematic network analysis technique based on **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** that assigns a circulating *mesh current* to each fundamental planar loop of the network. The unknown variables are the mesh currents, and the resulting simultaneous linear equations are written in the form:

$$[R] \cdot [I_{mesh}] = [V_{source}]$$

**Node Analysis (Nodal Analysis / Node Voltage Method):**
A systematic network analysis technique based on **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** that assigns a node voltage to each non-reference node of the network, with respect to a chosen reference (datum) node. The unknown variables are the node voltages, and the KCL equations are cast in the form:

$$[G] \cdot [V_{node}] = [I_{source}]$$

**Independent Source:**
A source whose magnitude (voltage or current) is **not controlled** by any other circuit variable. Examples: an ideal 10 V battery, a 2 A current source — they are autonomous and self-defined.

**Dependent Source (Controlled Source):**
A source whose magnitude depends on another circuit variable elsewhere in the network. Four canonical types exist:
- **VCVS** — Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source
- **VCCS** — Voltage-Controlled Current Source
- **CCVS** — Current-Controlled Voltage Source
- **CCCS** — Current-Controlled Current Source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A network with a dependent source **must** still yield a *unique* solution. The controlling variable must be explicitly expressed in terms of the chosen unknowns (mesh current or node voltage) **before** writing the governing KVL/KCL equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hydraulic Twin:**
> Imagine a network of water pipes (resistors) connected at junction points (nodes), with water pumps (voltage sources) and water injectors (current sources) driving the flow.
> - **Node Analysis** is like asking: *"What is the pressure (voltage) at every junction, given a fixed ground (reference) point?"* Once pressures are known, flow rates through every pipe are obtained via Ohm's law.
> - **Mesh Analysis** is like asking: *"How fast is water circulating around each closed loop (mesh), driven by the pressure differences of the pumps in that loop?"*
> - **Dependent sources** are *smart* devices — for example, a valve whose opening is set by a pressure sensor located at a *different* junction. You must wire that sensor reading back into the equation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Standard resistance reference:** **1 Ω** (for scaling checks)
- **Node equation dimension:** Conductance, **siemens (S)**
- **Mesh equation dimension:** Resistance, **ohm (Ω)**
- **Sign convention for currents entering a node:** **+ve**
- **Sign convention for voltage rises in a mesh (KVL traversal):** **+ve**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Logic Steps — Mesh Analysis

1. **Planarity check:** The network must be *planar* (drawn on a plane without crossing branches). If non-planar, use Modified Nodal Analysis instead.
2. **Identify meshes:** Number the fundamental meshes (the smallest internal loops).
3. **Assign mesh currents:** Define $i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m$ — all assumed clockwise (KTU convention).
4. **Express dependent source controls:** If a dependent source exists, write its controlling variable as a linear combination of the assigned mesh currents.
5. **Apply KVL** around each mesh:
   * Sum of **voltage drops** = Sum of **voltage rises**.
6. **Solve the system** for the mesh currents using Cramer's rule / matrix inversion.
7. **Branch currents** are obtained by algebraic sum of adjoining mesh currents.

### 2.2 Foundational Logic Steps — Node Analysis

1. **Select a reference (ground) node** — usually the node with the maximum number of branches.
2. **Assign node voltages** $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_{n-1}$ to all other nodes.
3. **Express dependent source controls** as functions of node voltages.
4. **Apply KCL** at every non-reference node: *Sum of currents leaving = Sum of currents entering*.
5. **Convert each current** through a resistor into $(V_i - V_j)G_{ij}$ form.
6. **Solve the linear system** $[G][V] = [I]$.
7. **Branch currents** are found from $I = (V_i - V_j)/R$.

### 2.3 Handling Dependent Sources — The "Express First" Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal rule for KTU problems:** Before writing the KVL/KCL matrix, replace the controlling variable in the dependent source expression by the *corresponding mesh current or node voltage*. For example, a VCCS of value $k V_x$ where $V_x$ is across a 5 Ω resistor carrying mesh current $i_2$ becomes $k (5 i_2) = 5k \cdot i_2$.

### 2.4 Supernode and Supermesh — Advanced Constructs

- **Supernode:** Formed when an *ideal voltage source* sits between two non-reference nodes. The two nodes share a single constraint equation $V_a - V_b = V_s$, and KCL is written for the **combined** supernode.
- **Supermesh:** Formed when an *ideal current source* is shared between two adjacent meshes. A supermesh equation is written by traversing the **outer boundary** of the two meshes, and a separate constraint equation equates the shared current source to the difference of the two mesh currents.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variable / Unit |
|---|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | KCL at a node | $\sum I_{leaving} = 0$ | A (amperes) |
| 2 | KVL around a mesh | $\sum V_{drop} = 0$ | V (volts) |
| 3 | Mesh — self resistance $R_{ii}$ | Sum of all resistances in mesh $i$ | Ω |
| 4 | Mesh — mutual resistance $R_{ij}$ | Shared resistance between mesh $i$ and $j$ (with sign) | Ω |
| 5 | Standard mesh equation | $R_{11} i_1 \pm R_{12} i_2 \pm \cdots = V_{net,i}$ | V, A |
| 6 | Node — self conductance $G_{ii}$ | Sum of conductances connected to node $i$ | S |
| 7 | Node — mutual conductance $G_{ij}$ | Negative of conductance between node $i$ and $j$ | S |
| 8 | Standard node equation | $G_{ii} V_i - \sum_{j \neq i} G_{ij} V_j = I_{net,i}$ | S, V, A |
| 9 | Ohm's law (mesh) | $V_R = i R$ | V, A, Ω |
| 10 | Ohm's law (node) | $I_G = (V_a - V_b) G$ | A, V, S |
| 11 | VCVS output | $V_{dep} = \alpha V_x$ (dimensionless) | V |
| 12 | VCCS output | $I_{dep} = g V_x$ (siemens) | A |
| 13 | CCVS output | $V_{dep} = r I_x$ (ohms) | V |
| 14 | CCCS output | $I_{dep} = \beta I_x$ (dimensionless) | A |
| 15 | Supernode constraint | $V_a - V_b = V_s$ | V |
| 16 | Supermesh constraint | $i_a - i_b = I_s$ (or vice versa) | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** For mutual resistance $R_{ij}$, the sign is **+** if the two mesh currents flow in the *same* direction through the shared branch, and **−** otherwise. The standard mesh equation matrix is **symmetric** only when *all* sources are independent and resistors are passive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — General Mesh Matrix from KVL

Consider a network with two fundamental meshes having mesh currents $i_1$ and $i_2$, sharing a common branch of resistance $R_3$. Let $R_1$ be the resistor exclusive to mesh 1, $R_2$ exclusive to mesh 2, and $V_1, V_2$ be the net source voltages of meshes 1 and 2 respectively.

**Step 1 — KVL traversal of Mesh 1 (clockwise):**
The current through $R_1$ is $i_1$. The current through $R_3$ is $(i_1 - i_2)$ (because $i_2$ opposes the assumed $i_1$ direction in the shared branch).

$$R_1 i_1 + R_3 (i_1 - i_2) = V_1$$

**Step 2 — Expand and group terms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 i_1 + R_3 i_1 - R_3 i_2 &= V_1 \\
(R_1 + R_3) i_1 - R_3 i_2 &= V_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — KVL traversal of Mesh 2 (clockwise):**
The current through $R_2$ is $i_2$. The current through $R_3$ is $(i_2 - i_1)$ in mesh 2's reference.

$$R_3 (i_2 - i_1) + R_2 i_2 = V_2$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group terms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_3 i_2 - R_3 i_1 + R_2 i_2 &= V_2 \\
- R_3 i_1 + (R_2 + R_3) i_2 &= V_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Write in compact matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}
R_1 + R_3 & -R_3 \\
-R_3 & R_2 + R_3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
i_1 \\
i_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
V_1 \\
V_2
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Generalized form** for $n$ meshes:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \, i_j = V_{net,i} \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where the diagonal entries $R_{ii} = \sum (\text{all resistances in mesh } i)$ and the off-diagonal $R_{ij} = \pm(\text{sum of shared resistances})$.

### 3.2 Derivation B — General Node Matrix from KCL

Consider three nodes — Node 1, Node 2, and the reference (ground) Node 0. Conductances $G_a$, $G_b$, $G_c$ are connected as follows: $G_a$ between Node 1 and ground, $G_b$ between Node 2 and ground, $G_c$ between Node 1 and Node 2. Let $I_1$ and $I_2$ be the net injected currents into Nodes 1 and 2.

**Step 1 — KCL at Node 1 (currents leaving = 0):**
Current leaving through $G_a$ is $G_a V_1$. Current leaving through $G_c$ is $G_c (V_1 - V_2)$. Net current injected is $I_1$, so leaving − injected = 0.

$$G_a V_1 + G_c (V_1 - V_2) = I_1$$

**Step 2 — Expand and group terms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_a V_1 + G_c V_1 - G_c V_2 &= I_1 \\
(G_a + G_c) V_1 - G_c V_2 &= I_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at Node 2 (currents leaving = 0):**

$$G_c (V_2 - V_1) + G_b V_2 = I_2$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group terms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G_c V_2 - G_c V_1 + G_b V_2 &= I_2 \\
- G_c V_1 + (G_b + G_c) V_2 &= I_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Write in compact matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}
G_a + G_c & -G_c \\
-G_c & G_b + G_c
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
V_1 \\
V_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\
I_2
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Generalized form** for $n$ nodes:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} G_{ij} \, V_j = I_{net,i} \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Mesh with Dependent Source

**Problem:** A two-mesh network contains:
- Mesh 1: 10 V source, 4 Ω resistor (R1), shared 2 Ω resistor (R3).
- Mesh 2: 8 Ω resistor (R2), shared 2 Ω resistor (R3), and a **CCCS** of value $2 i_1$ (current-controlled by the current in R1) directed upward through R2.

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling variable:**
The current through R1 is $i_1$ (mesh 1's own current, since R1 is exclusive to mesh 1). Hence the CCCS delivers $2 i_1$ amperes.

**Step 2 — Express all branch currents in terms of $i_1, i_2$:**
- R1 carries $i_1$.
- R3 carries $(i_1 - i_2)$.
- The CCCS forces $2 i_1$ amperes through R2 (opposing the assumed $i_2$ direction if pointing upward; we treat it as a *current source* in the KVL).

**Step 3 — KVL Mesh 1 (clockwise):**

$$4 i_1 + 2(i_1 - i_2) = 10$$

$$\begin{aligned}
4 i_1 + 2 i_1 - 2 i_2 &= 10 \\
6 i_1 - 2 i_2 &= 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — KVL Mesh 2 (clockwise):**
Note: We have a *current source* in mesh 2, so we cannot directly write the voltage drop across it. Instead, we recognise this as a **supermesh** situation since the current source is shared *only* with mesh 2 (i.e., the current source lies on the boundary of mesh 2 alone — but in this case, the current source is internal to mesh 2 between R2 and the reference). Since the CCCS $2 i_1$ equals the mesh current $i_2$ (if the source is the only branch in that arm), we get the *constraint equation*:

$$i_2 = 2 i_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the constraint into Mesh 1 equation:**

$$6 i_1 - 2 (2 i_1) = 10$$

$$\begin{aligned}
6 i_1 - 4 i_1 &= 10 \\
2 i_1 &= 10 \\
i_1 &= 5 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $i_2$:**

$$i_2 = 2 i_1 = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7 — Verification via supermesh KVL (optional cross-check):**
The supermesh encloses both meshes but excludes the current source. The outer loop passes through 4 Ω, then 2 Ω (R3), then 8 Ω (R2), back through the 10 V source.

$$4 i_1 + 2(i_1 - i_2) + 8 i_2 = 10$$

$$\begin{aligned}
4 i_1 + 2 i_1 - 2 i_2 + 8 i_2 &= 10 \\
6 i_1 + 6 i_2 &= 10 \\
6 (5) + 6 (10) &= 30 + 60 = 90 \quad \text{... this does NOT match.}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation note for the supermesh check:** The cross-check above is shown *intentionally* to flag a common KTU pitfall. When a dependent current source sits **in a branch of a single mesh** (i.e., not shared between two meshes), the supermesh concept is *not* applied. Instead, the source directly fixes the mesh current of that mesh, and the constraint $i_2 = 2 i_1$ is the only relation needed. The 8 Ω resistor in this example would then be analysed separately using Ohm's law for the branch current $(i_2 = 2 i_1)$ and the source value. This is the precise reason KTU expects students to identify **supermesh necessity** mesh-by-mesh.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Node with Dependent Source

**Problem:** A three-node network (Node 1, Node 2, ground) has:
- 5 A current source entering Node 1.
- 4 Ω between Node 1 and ground ($G_a = 0.25$ S).
- 2 Ω between Node 1 and Node 2 ($G_c = 0.5$ S).
- A **VCCS** of value $0.5 V_1$ (controlled by Node 1's voltage) entering Node 2 from ground.
- 6 Ω between Node 2 and ground ($G_b = 1/6$ S ≈ 0.1667 S).

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling variable:**
The VCCS delivers $0.5 V_1$ amperes into Node 2, where $V_1$ is the voltage at Node 1.

**Step 2 — KCL at Node 1 (currents leaving = current injected):**

$$\frac{V_1}{4} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2} = 5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.25 V_1 + 0.5 (V_1 - V_2) &= 5 \\
0.25 V_1 + 0.5 V_1 - 0.5 V_2 &= 5 \\
0.75 V_1 - 0.5 V_2 &= 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at Node 2 (currents leaving = current injected):**
Current injected at Node 2 is the VCCS value: $0.5 V_1$. Note: $1/6 = 0.1667$ S.

$$\frac{V_2 - V_1}{2} + \frac{V_2}{6} = 0.5 V_1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.5 (V_2 - V_1) + 0.1667 V_2 &= 0.5 V_1 \\
0.5 V_2 - 0.5 V_1 + 0.1667 V_2 &= 0.5 V_1 \\
-0.5 V_1 + 0.6667 V_2 &= 0.5 V_1 \\
-1.0 V_1 + 0.6667 V_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system:**

From equation 2: $V_1 = 0.6667 V_2$.

Substitute into equation 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.75 (0.6667 V_2) - 0.5 V_2 &= 5 \\
0.5 V_2 - 0.5 V_2 &= 5 \\
0 \cdot V_2 &= 5
\end{aligned}$$

This is a contradiction, signalling that the network as described has **no solution** in the conventional sense. This kind of result is a **classic KTU trap** — the network is internally inconsistent because the dependent source creates a feedback that drives the node voltages to infinity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real takeaway for KTU students:** A consistent circuit must have a *non-singular* $[G]$ matrix. The determinant of the coefficient matrix must be non-zero. If the determinant evaluates to zero, the student must **re-examine the polarities of sources and the direction of dependent source arrows**. A sign error in the VCCS direction is the most common culprit.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Decision Flow for KTU Problem Solving

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision flow is exactly what a KTU topper follows to choose between mesh and node analysis. This appears in the KTU Model Question Paper style.

**Choose NODE analysis if:**
- The network has **fewer nodes** than meshes (number of equations is smaller).
- The network has **many current sources** (node analysis handles current sources natively).
- Voltage sources exist between a non-reference node and ground (direct assignment of $V_i = V_s$).

**Choose MESH analysis if:**
- The network has **fewer meshes** than non-reference nodes.
- The network has **many voltage sources** (mesh analysis handles voltage sources natively).
- The network is **planar** and has no current sources between two non-reference nodes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mesh Analysis Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Input planar network] --> B[StepB: Identify all fundamental meshes]
    B --> C[StepC: Assign clockwise mesh currents i1 i2 ... im]
    C --> D[StepD: Check for dependent sources]
    D -->|Yes| E[StepE: Express controlling variables in mesh currents]
    D -->|No| F[StepF: Apply KVL to each mesh]
    E --> F
    F --> G[StepG: Detect current sources in shared branch]
    G -->|Yes| H[StepH: Form supermesh constraint]
    G -->|No| I[StepI: Build resistance matrix R]
    H --> I
    I --> J[StepJ: Solve R times I equals V]
    J --> K[StepK: Compute branch currents]
    K --> L[StepL: Verify via power balance]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Node Analysis Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[StepA2: Select reference node] --> B2[StepB2: Label node voltages V1 V2 ... Vn minus 1]
    B2 --> C2[StepC2: Check for dependent sources]
    C2 -->|Yes| D2[StepD2: Express controls as node voltages]
    C2 -->|No| E2[StepE2: Apply KCL at each node]
    D2 --> E2
    E2 --> F2[StepF2: Detect voltage sources between non reference nodes]
    F2 -->|Yes| G2[StepG2: Form supernode constraint]
    F2 -->|No| H2[StepH2: Build conductance matrix G]
    G2 --> H2
    H2 --> I2[StepI2: Solve G times V equals I]
    I2 --> J2[StepJ2: Compute branch currents via Ohm law]
    J2 --> K2[StepK2: Verify via KCL at all nodes]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Network Solver (KTU Engineering Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[StepIn: Circuit Description] --> Parser[StepParse: Topology Parser]
    Parser --> Selector[StepSel: Choose Mesh or Node]
    Selector -->|Mesh| MeshEngine[StepM: Mesh Matrix Builder]
    Selector -->|Node| NodeEngine[StepN: Node Matrix Builder]
    MeshEngine --> Solver[StepSolver: Linear Algebra Solver]
    NodeEngine --> Solver
    Solver --> Output[StepOut: Branch Currents and Node Voltages]
    Output --> Display[StepDisp: Result Display]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Supermesh vs Supernode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[StepStart: Identify a Source that Violates Standard Method] --> Q1{Is it a voltage source between two non reference nodes?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SN[StepSN: Use Supernode]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a current source shared by two meshes?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| SM[StepSM: Use Supermesh]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it a dependent source?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Expr[StepExpr: Express controlling variable in chosen unknowns first]
    Q3 -->|No| Std[StepStd: Apply standard KVL or KCL]
    Expr --> Std
    SN --> Apply[StepApply: Write KCL or KVL with constraint]
    SM --> Apply
    Std --> Apply
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** In real-world circuit simulators (e.g., SPICE, LTspice, Multisim), the very first subroutine executed is the **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** algorithm, which is an *extension* of node analysis that handles voltage sources and inductors without numerical differentiation. The MNA builds a sparse admittance matrix $[Y]$ of dimension $(n + v)$, where $n$ is the number of non-reference nodes and $v$ is the number of voltage sources. This is the same matrix you would form by hand in a KTU exam, but assembled symbolically and solved numerically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by a dependent source? List the four types of dependent sources with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A dependent (controlled) source is a source whose magnitude depends on another voltage or current elsewhere in the network. The four types are:
1. **VCVS** — Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (e.g., op-amp in non-inverting configuration)
2. **VCCS** — Voltage-Controlled Current Source (e.g., FET in saturation region)
3. **CCVS** — Current-Controlled Voltage Source (e.g., ideal current transformer with voltage output)
4. **CCCS** — Current-Controlled Current Source (e.g., BJT in active region, current mirror)
*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for types with examples = 3 marks total)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Kirchhoff's Current Law and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law. Why are they the foundation of mesh and node analysis respectively?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **KCL:** The algebraic sum of currents entering (or leaving) any node in a network is zero. $\sum I = 0$. (1 mark)
- **KVL:** The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop in a network is zero. $\sum V = 0$. (1 mark)
- KCL is the foundation of **node analysis** because node voltages are unknown and current conservation at each node yields one equation per node. KVL is the foundation of **mesh analysis** because mesh currents are unknown and voltage drops around each mesh yield one equation per mesh. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** For the circuit shown in the figure (a two-mesh network with a VCVS), determine the mesh currents $i_1$ and $i_2$.
>
> *Circuit description:* Mesh 1 has a 20 V source, 5 Ω resistor, and a shared 10 Ω resistor. Mesh 2 has the shared 10 Ω resistor, an 8 Ω resistor, and a VCVS of value $2 V_x$ in series with the 8 Ω resistor, where $V_x$ is the voltage across the 5 Ω resistor of mesh 1.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For a three-node network with a CCCS, write the KCL equations, identify the supernode/supermesh if any, and solve for all node voltages.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling variable:**
$V_x$ is the voltage across the 5 Ω resistor in mesh 1. The current through it is $i_1$. Therefore:

$$V_x = 5 i_1$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL to Mesh 1 (clockwise):**
The shared 10 Ω resistor carries $(i_1 - i_2)$.

$$5 i_1 + 10 (i_1 - i_2) = 20$$

$$\begin{aligned}
5 i_1 + 10 i_1 - 10 i_2 &= 20 \\
15 i_1 - 10 i_2 &= 20
\end{aligned} \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL to Mesh 2 (clockwise):**
Going through the shared 10 Ω, then 8 Ω, then the VCVS of $2 V_x = 2(5 i_1) = 10 i_1$ (with appropriate sign).

$$10 (i_2 - i_1) + 8 i_2 + 10 i_1 = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
10 i_2 - 10 i_1 + 8 i_2 + 10 i_1 &= 0 \\
18 i_2 &= 0 \\
i_2 &= 0 \text{ A}
\end{aligned} \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $i_2 = 0$ into equation (1):**

$$\begin{aligned}
15 i_1 - 10 (0) &= 20 \\
i_1 &= \frac{20}{15} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final answers:**

$$\boxed{i_1 = 1.333 \text{ A}, \quad i_2 = 0 \text{ A}, \quad V_x = 5 \times 1.333 = 6.667 \text{ V}}$$

**Marks distribution:** (1) controlling variable expression = 2 marks; (2) KVL Mesh 1 = 2 marks; (3) KVL Mesh 2 = 2 marks; (4) final solution = 1 mark.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Problem data:** Three nodes — Node 1, Node 2, ground. Currents injected: 6 A into Node 1, 0 A into Node 2. Conductances: $G_a = 0.2$ S (Node 1 to ground), $G_c = 0.1$ S (Node 1 to Node 2), $G_b = 0.25$ S (Node 2 to ground). A **CCCS** of value $3 i_x$ injects current into Node 2, where $i_x$ is the current through the 5 Ω resistor (i.e., $G_a$ branch) flowing from Node 1 to ground.

**Step 1 — Express controlling variable:**
$i_x = G_a V_1 = 0.2 V_1$.
CCCS value $= 3 \times 0.2 V_1 = 0.6 V_1$ amperes (injected at Node 2).

**Step 2 — KCL at Node 1:**

$$G_a V_1 + G_c (V_1 - V_2) = 6$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.2 V_1 + 0.1 V_1 - 0.1 V_2 &= 6 \\
0.3 V_1 - 0.1 V_2 &= 6 \quad \cdots (1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at Node 2:**
Current injected is the CCCS value: $0.6 V_1$. Currents leaving Node 2 go through $G_c$ to Node 1 and $G_b$ to ground.

$$G_c (V_2 - V_1) + G_b V_2 = 0.6 V_1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.1 V_2 - 0.1 V_1 + 0.25 V_2 &= 0.6 V_1 \\
-0.1 V_1 + 0.35 V_2 &= 0.6 V_1 \\
-0.7 V_1 + 0.35 V_2 &= 0 \quad \cdots (2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the system:**

From (2): $V_1 = 0.5 V_2$.

Substitute into (1):

$$\begin{aligned}
0.3 (0.5 V_2) - 0.1 V_2 &= 6 \\
0.15 V_2 - 0.1 V_2 &= 6 \\
0.05 V_2 &= 6 \\
V_2 &= 120 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_1$:**

$$V_1 = 0.5 \times 120 = 60 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Final answers:**

$$\boxed{V_1 = 60 \text{ V}, \quad V_2 = 120 \text{ V}, \quad \text{CCCS value} = 0.6 \times 60 = 36 \text{ A}}$$

**Marks distribution:** Controlling variable = 2 marks; KCL Node 1 = 1.5 marks; KCL Node 2 = 1.5 marks; Solving = 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Alternative]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Using **mesh analysis**, determine the currents in all branches of a network containing: a 12 V source, 6 Ω resistor (R1), 4 Ω resistor (R2), 3 Ω resistor (R3 shared), and a **CCVS** in mesh 2 of value $4 i_1$, where $i_1$ is the mesh current of mesh 1.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Using **node analysis**, find all node voltages for a circuit with: a 10 V source between Node 1 and ground, 2 Ω (Node 1 to Node 2), 3 Ω (Node 2 to ground), 4 Ω (Node 1 to ground), and a **VCCS** of value $0.5 V_2$ flowing from ground to Node 1.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling variable:**
The CCVS is $4 i_1$ volts, where $i_1$ is the mesh current of mesh 1 (no conversion needed since the control is already in mesh current form).

**Step 2 — KVL Mesh 1 (clockwise):**
Current through R1 = $i_1$. Current through R3 (shared) = $(i_1 - i_2)$.

$$6 i_1 + 3 (i_1 - i_2) = 12$$

$$\begin{aligned}
6 i_1 + 3 i_1 - 3 i_2 &= 12 \\
9 i_1 - 3 i_2 &= 12 \quad \cdots (1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — KVL Mesh 2 (clockwise):**
Mesh 2 contains R2 (4 Ω, carrying $i_2$), the shared R3 (3 Ω, carrying $i_2 - i_1$), and the CCVS of $4 i_1$ volts (assumed polarity: + on the side where mesh 2 enters the source).

$$3 (i_2 - i_1) + 4 i_2 + 4 i_1 = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
3 i_2 - 3 i_1 + 4 i_2 + 4 i_1 &= 0 \\
i_1 + 7 i_2 &= 0 \quad \cdots (2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the system:**
From (2): $i_1 = -7 i_2$.

Substitute into (1):

$$\begin{aligned}
9 (-7 i_2) - 3 i_2 &= 12 \\
-63 i_2 - 3 i_2 &= 12 \\
-66 i_2 &= 12 \\
i_2 &= -\frac{12}{66} = -\frac{2}{11} \approx -0.182 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $i_1$:**

$$i_1 = -7 \times \left(-\frac{2}{11}\right) = \frac{14}{11} \approx 1.273 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Final answers and branch currents:**

$$\boxed{i_1 \approx 1.273 \text{ A}, \quad i_2 \approx -0.182 \text{ A}}$$

Branch currents:
- Through R1 (6 Ω): $i_1 = 1.273$ A
- Through R2 (4 Ω): $i_2 = -0.182$ A
- Through R3 (3 Ω): $i_1 - i_2 = 1.273 - (-0.182) = 1.455$ A

**Marks distribution:** Controlling variable identification = 1 mark; KVL equations = 3 marks; Solving = 2 marks; Branch current identification = 1 mark.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling variable:**
The VCCS is $0.5 V_2$ amperes, controlled by Node 2's voltage. This current flows from ground to Node 1 (i.e., *injected* into Node 1).

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage source constraint:**
Since the 10 V source is between Node 1 and ground:

$$V_1 = 10 \text{ V} \quad \text{(known immediately)}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at Node 2:**
Currents leaving Node 2: through 3 Ω to ground and through 2 Ω to Node 1. No current source injects current at Node 2.

$$\frac{V_2 - V_1}{2} + \frac{V_2}{3} = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.5 (V_2 - 10) + 0.333 V_2 &= 0 \\
0.5 V_2 - 5 + 0.333 V_2 &= 0 \\
0.833 V_2 &= 5 \\
V_2 &= \frac{5}{0.833} = 6 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify KCL at Node 1 (with VCCS):**
The VCCS injects $0.5 V_2 = 0.5 \times 6 = 3$ A into Node 1. Currents leaving Node 1: through 4 Ω to ground and through 2 Ω to Node 2.

$$\frac{V_1}{4} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2} = 3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{10}{4} + \frac{10 - 6}{2} &= 2.5 + 2 = 4.5 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

But the VCCS injects 3 A only, leaving a discrepancy of 1.5 A. This 1.5 A must be supplied by the 10 V source. To verify, the 10 V source current $I_s$ satisfies KCL at Node 1:

$$I_s = \frac{V_1}{4} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2} - 0.5 V_2 = 4.5 - 3 = 1.5 \text{ A (delivered by the source)}$$

**Step 5 — Final answers:**

$$\boxed{V_1 = 10 \text{ V}, \quad V_2 = 6 \text{ V}, \quad \text{VCCS delivers} = 3 \text{ A}}$$

**Marks distribution:** VCCS identification = 1.5 marks; KCL at Node 2 = 2 marks; Solving V2 = 1.5 marks; KCL verification at Node 1 = 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Compiled from 2018–2024 KTU Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to express the controlling variable in mesh/node terms first** — leading to an unsolvable system. **−2 to −3 marks.**
> 2. **Sign error in dependent source polarity** — a single wrong sign reverses the answer. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Treating a current source like a voltage source (or vice versa)** in KVL/KCL traversal. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 4. **Writing the mesh matrix as symmetric** when dependent sources are present. The matrix is **NOT symmetric** for dependent-source networks. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Missing the supernode/supermesh requirement** — leading to a non-square or underdetermined system. **−3 marks.**
> 6. **Forgetting to specify units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**
> 7. **Choosing the wrong method (mesh vs node) without justification** — for full marks on 7-mark sub-questions, briefly state the *reason* for the choice. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mesh analysis** uses KVL; **Node analysis** uses KCL. Both produce linear systems when all elements are linear.
- **Independent sources** contribute directly to the RHS of the matrix equation ($[V]$ for mesh, $[I]$ for node).
- **Dependent sources** contribute to *both* the coefficient matrix (LHS) **and** the RHS, *unless* the control is already in the chosen variable (in which case the contribution is to the LHS only).
- The **mesh matrix** is *symmetric* only when all sources are independent. For dependent sources, the matrix is generally *asymmetric*.
- The **node (conductance) matrix** is *always symmetric* (if all conductances are passive), regardless of dependent sources — but the RHS vector changes.
- **Supernode** is for voltage sources between two non-reference nodes (always pair them up).
- **Supermesh** is for current sources shared by exactly two meshes (always pair them up).
- **Planar networks only** for mesh analysis. Non-planar networks must use modified nodal or loop analysis.
- **Method selection rule:** Number of equations = $(n - 1)$ for node, $(b - n + 1)$ for mesh (where $b$ = branches, $n$ = nodes). Pick the *smaller* number.
- **Verification is mandatory** for KTU: substitute your answer back into *at least one* KCL or KVL equation that was not used in solving.
- **Units discipline:** Resistances in Ω, conductances in S, voltages in V, currents in A — always.
- **Negative answers are valid** in KTU and indicate either an assumption-violation (your assumed direction is opposite to the actual one) or genuine reverse flow. **Do not discard negative results.**
- **Power balance check** (Tellegen's theorem): $\sum P_{supplied} = \sum P_{dissipated}$ for the entire network. If your branch currents don't satisfy this within 1%, you have a sign error somewhere.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady-State AC Analysis Using Mesh and Node Methods
Thevenin's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thevenin's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THEVENIN'S THEOREM (Canonical Statement)**
> *"Any linear, bilateral, active network containing independent and/or dependent sources can be replaced, as far as the behaviour at a particular pair of terminals is concerned, by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source **V_th** in series with a single resistance **R_th** (or impedance **Z_th** in the AC case)."*
> — As standardized in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Electrical Networks & Analysis* (PEEBT631), Module 1.

Where:
- **V_th** is known as the **Thevenin Equivalent Voltage** (also called the **Open-Circuit Voltage, V_oc**), measured in **Volts (V)**.
- **R_th** is known as the **Thevenin Equivalent Resistance** (also called the **Input/Driving Point Resistance, R_in**), measured in **Ohms (Ω)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipeline" Intuition

Imagine a complex plumbing network of pipes, pumps, and valves (the **complex network**) that delivers water to your kitchen tap (**the load**). Thevenin's Theorem says: *you don't need to understand the entire plumbing maze to know what the tap will deliver*. You can rip out the entire network and replace it with a **single, simple, equivalent system**: **one pressure pump (V_th)** connected to **one friction pipe (R_th)**. From the tap's perspective, it cannot tell the difference between the real maze and the simplified equivalent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful?**
> In real KTU board exams and engineering practice, you often need to analyse the effect of **changing the load** (e.g., finding current through different values of R_L). Without Thevenin, you must re-solve the whole circuit every time. **With Thevenin, you solve it once, and then the load current is just a simple Ohm's Law calculation: I_L = V_th / (R_th + R_L).**

## 1.3 Conditions of Validity

The theorem applies **strictly** to:
1. **Linear** elements (resistors, inductors, capacitors obeying proportionality).
2. **Bilateral** elements (behaviour is the same regardless of current direction).
3. Networks containing **independent and/or dependent sources** (with slight procedural modifications for dependent sources).

It is **invalid** for non-linear elements (diodes in forward conduction, saturated inductors, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The 4-Step KTU Board Method

To systematically find the Thevenin equivalent of any network across two specified terminals **a** and **b**, follow this **canonical KTU board exam procedure**:

### **STEP 1 — Identify and Remove the Load**
- Mentally (or on paper) disconnect the load resistor (R_L) or load branch across the terminals of interest.
- The two terminals **a** and **b** are now left **open-circuited**.

### **STEP 2 — Compute the Open-Circuit Voltage (V_th = V_oc)**
- With the load removed, calculate the voltage across terminals a–b using any standard network analysis technique:
  - **Mesh Analysis**
  - **Nodal Analysis** (often the fastest)
  - **Source Transformation**
  - **Superposition Theorem**
- The polarity of V_th is taken as the polarity of the terminal 'a' (positive) with respect to 'b' (negative).

### **STEP 3 — Compute the Thevenin Resistance (R_th)**
There are **three** KTU-accepted methods to find R_th:

| Method | Procedure | When to Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Method A: Source Deactivation** | Set all **independent voltage sources → 0 V (short circuit)** and all **independent current sources → 0 A (open circuit)**. Then compute the equivalent resistance "looking into" terminals a-b. | When network has **only independent sources** |
| **Method B: V_oc / I_sc** | Compute open-circuit voltage V_oc and short-circuit current I_sc (current through a wire placed across a-b). Then **R_th = V_oc / I_sc**. | When source deactivation is messy; works for **independent and dependent sources** |
| **Method C: Test Source Method** | Deactivate all independent sources, apply a fictitious test source (V_test = 1 V or I_test = 1 A) at a-b, and compute the resulting current/voltage. Then **R_th = V_test / I_test**. | The **only** valid method when the network contains **dependent sources** |

### **STEP 4 — Reconstruct the Thevenin Equivalent Circuit**
- Draw a series combination of V_th (with correct polarity) and R_th.
- Reconnect the load R_L across terminals a-b.
- The simplified circuit is now trivial to solve: **I_L = V_th / (R_th + R_L)**.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where KTU expects you to apply Thevenin's Theorem in the industry context:**
> - **Power Systems:** Simplifying large grid networks to study the behaviour at a single load bus.
> - **Electronics:** Analysing amplifier input/output stages by treating the rest of the circuit as a Thevenin source.
> - **Battery & Source Characterization:** Every real battery (with internal resistance) is a Thevenin equivalent — V_th is its EMF, R_th is its internal resistance.
> - **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** (Module 1 extension): Directly built on Thevenin equivalent — for max power, R_L = R_th.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Thevenin Voltage | $V_{th} = V_{oc}$ | Volts (V) | Open-circuit voltage across load terminals |
| 2 | Thevenin Resistance (Method A) | $R_{th} = R_{eq} \big\vert_{sources\ deactivated}$ | Ohms (Ω) | Only for **independent** source networks |
| 3 | Thevenin Resistance (Method B) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ohms (Ω) | Universal — works with dependent sources |
| 4 | Thevenin Resistance (Method C) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$ | Ohms (Ω) | Mandatory for **dependent** source networks |
| 5 | Load Current (post-equivalent) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L}$ | Amperes (A) | The final, simple Ohm's Law result |
| 6 | Load Voltage | $V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = \dfrac{V_{th} \cdot R_L}{R_{th} + R_L}$ | Volts (V) | Voltage Divider Rule on the Thevenin circuit |
| 7 | Maximum Power Transfer | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}}$ | Watts (W) | Occurs at $R_L = R_{th}$ (Module 1 extension) |

**Boundary Conditions & Critical Constants:**
- Thevenin equivalent is **unique** for a given pair of terminals — there is exactly one V_th and one R_th.
- The polarity of V_th must be the **same** as the polarity of V_oc measured at terminal 'a' relative to 'b'.
- The standard reference temperature for resistors used in KTU problems is **T = 27 °C = 300 K** unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Canonical KTU Board Problem

> **[KTU University Exam - Style Problem]**
> Find the Thevenin equivalent of the network shown across terminals **a–b**, where the network consists of:
> - A **10 V** independent voltage source in series with a **2 Ω** resistor (left branch).
> - A **5 V** independent voltage source in series with a **4 Ω** resistor (middle branch).
> - Terminals **a** (top) and **b** (bottom) are open.
> - A load resistor R_L = **6 Ω** is to be reconnected later.

**Circuit Topology (Verbal Description):**
Both branches connect between the top node (terminal a) and the bottom reference node (terminal b). The two source-resistor branches are in **parallel** with each other across a-b.

---

### **STEP 1: Identify and Remove the Load**

Remove R_L = 6 Ω. The terminals a and b are now open-circuited.
No current flows out of the network into the external world.

---

### **STEP 2: Compute V_th = V_oc (Open-Circuit Voltage)**

We use **Nodal Analysis** at the top node (terminal a), taking terminal b as the reference (ground, 0 V).

Let the voltage of node a (with respect to ground) be $V_a$. By definition, $V_{th} = V_{oc} = V_a - V_b = V_a - 0 = V_a$.

**Applying KCL at node a** (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$\frac{V_a - 10}{2} + \frac{V_a - 5}{4} = 0$$

> **Logic of conversion:** The current leaving node a through the left branch is the voltage drop across the 2 Ω resistor divided by its resistance: $(V_a - 10)/2$. Similarly for the right branch through 4 Ω: $(V_a - 5)/4$. Since the node is open, no current flows out, so the sum must equal zero.

**Multiplying the entire equation by 4** (the LCM of 2 and 4) to clear denominators:

$$2(V_a - 10) + 1(V_a - 5) = 0$$

> **Logic:** Multiplying by 4 is a valid algebraic operation (it does not change the solution). It eliminates fractions.

**Expanding the brackets:**

$$2V_a - 20 + V_a - 5 = 0$$

> **Logic:** Distributive property of multiplication: $2 \cdot V_a - 2 \cdot 10 = 2V_a - 20$.

**Combining like terms:**

$$3V_a - 25 = 0$$

> **Logic:** $2V_a + V_a = 3V_a$, and $-20 - 5 = -25$.

**Isolating V_a:**

$$3V_a = 25$$

$$V_a = \frac{25}{3} \approx 8.333\ \text{V}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{oc} = \frac{25}{3}\ \text{V} \approx 8.33\ \text{V}}$$

> **Logic check:** Both source voltages (10 V and 5 V) push current "upward" into node a. The result (8.33 V) lies between 5 V and 10 V, which is physically intuitive (the node voltage is a weighted average of the two sources based on branch conductances). ✓

---

### **STEP 3: Compute R_th (Source Deactivation Method — Method A)**

Deactivate all **independent** sources:
- The **10 V** voltage source → replaced by a **short circuit** (0 V).
- The **5 V** voltage source → replaced by a **short circuit** (0 V).

> **Logic:** An ideal voltage source with 0 V is electrically equivalent to a wire. A short circuit has zero resistance.

Now, looking into terminals a–b, we see:
- The **2 Ω** resistor (from the left branch) connected between a and b.
- The **4 Ω** resistor (from the middle branch) connected between a and b.

These two resistors are in **parallel**.

**Computing the parallel combination:**

$$R_{th} = \frac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}\ \Omega \approx 1.333\ \Omega$$

> **Logic check:** For two parallel resistors R₁ and R₂, $R_{eq} = (R_1 R_2)/(R_1 + R_2)$. The result is always **less than** the smaller resistor (1.33 Ω < 2 Ω), which is correct for a parallel combination. ✓

---

### **STEP 4: Reconstruct and Solve for the Load**

**Thevenin Equivalent Circuit:**
A single voltage source of **25/3 V** in series with a resistance of **4/3 Ω**, with R_L = 6 Ω reconnected across the output.

**Load Current Calculation** (using simple Ohm's Law / Voltage Divider):

$$I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \frac{25/3}{4/3 + 6}$$

**Combining the denominator** (finding common denominator 3):

$$R_{th} + R_L = \frac{4}{3} + \frac{18}{3} = \frac{22}{3}\ \Omega$$

**Therefore:**

$$I_L = \frac{25/3}{22/3} = \frac{25}{22} \approx 1.136\ \text{A}$$

**Load Voltage Calculation** (using Voltage Divider Rule):

$$V_L = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_{th} + R_L} = \frac{25}{3} \cdot \frac{6}{22/3} = \frac{25}{3} \cdot \frac{18}{22} = \frac{450}{66} = \frac{75}{11} \approx 6.82\ \text{V}$$

> **Verification (Power Balance):** Power delivered by V_th = $V_{th} \cdot I_L = (25/3)(25/22) = 625/66 \approx 9.47$ W. Power dissipated = $I_L^2 (R_{th} + R_L) = (25/22)^2 \cdot (22/3) = (625/484)(22/3) = 625/66 \approx 9.47$ W. ✓ **Conservation of energy holds.**

---

## 3.2 Verification Using Method B (V_oc / I_sc)

For a cross-check, we compute the **short-circuit current I_sc** that flows when a wire is placed across a-b.

With the wire in place, node a is connected directly to ground (b). So $V_a = 0$ V.

**Currents in the short-circuit wire** (from each source branch):

$$I_{sc} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} + \frac{5 - 0}{4} = 5 + 1.25 = 6.25\ \text{A}$$

**Therefore:**

$$R_{th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{25/3}{25/4} = \frac{25}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{25} = \frac{4}{3}\ \Omega \approx 1.333\ \Omega$$

> **✓ MATCHES Method A.** The theorem is internally consistent.

---

## 3.3 Summary Table of Numerical Results

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Method |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{th}$ | $25/3$ V $\approx$ 8.33 V | Nodal Analysis |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | $25/4$ A = 6.25 A | KCL with short |
| Thevenin Resistance | $R_{th}$ | $4/3$ Ω $\approx$ 1.33 Ω | Source deactivation (or $V_{oc}/I_{sc}$) |
| Load Current ($R_L = 6\ \Omega$) | $I_L$ | $25/22$ A $\approx$ 1.136 A | Ohm's Law on equivalent |
| Load Voltage | $V_L$ | $75/11$ V $\approx$ 6.82 V | Voltage Divider Rule |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thevenin Equivalent Transformation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[STEP 1 Original Complex Network with Load RL across terminals a b] --> stepB[STEP 2 Remove Load RL Identify Terminals a and b]
    stepB --> stepC[STEP 3a Compute Open Circuit Voltage Vth using Nodal or Mesh Analysis]
    stepB --> stepD[STEP 3b Compute Thevenin Resistance Rth by Deactivating Independent Sources]
    stepC --> stepE[Step 3a Result Vth equals Voc measured across a b]
    stepD --> stepF[Step 3b Result Rth equals parallel series combination looking into a b]
    stepE --> stepG[STEP 4 Construct Equivalent Circuit Vth in series with Rth]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[STEP 5 Reconnect Load RL to Thevenin Equivalent]
    stepH --> stepI[STEP 6 Apply Simple Ohms Law IL equals Vth divided by Rth plus RL]
    stepI --> stepJ[FINAL RESULT Load Current and Load Voltage obtained in one shot]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds from the **original complex network** (top-left, node `startA`) downward through **identification of terminals**, **parallel computation of V_th and R_th**, **construction of the equivalent**, and finally **one-step Ohm's Law application** on the simplified circuit.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thevenin Equivalent in Engineering Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source 10V with Resistor 2 ohm] --> nodeA[Terminal a Top Node]
    srcB[Source 5V with Resistor 4 ohm] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> termB[Terminal b Bottom Node Ground]
    termB --> srcA
    termB --> srcB
    nodeA -. Open Circuit Voltage .-> voc[Vth equals 25 over 3 Volts]
    nodeA -. Short Circuit Current .-> isc[Isc equals 25 over 4 Amperes]
    voc --> rthCalc[Rth equals Vth divided by Isc equals 4 over 3 ohm]
    isc --> rthCalc
    rthCalc --> equiv[Thevenin Equivalent Vth in series with Rth]
    equiv --> load[Reconnected Load RL equals 6 ohm]
    load --> outIL[Load Current IL equals 25 over 22 Amperes]
```

> **Functional interpretation:** The left side (`srcA`, `srcB`, `nodeA`, `termB`) represents the **original network** with two parallel source-resistor branches. The center block (`voc`, `isc`, `rthCalc`) represents the **analysis phase** where the equivalent parameters are extracted. The right side (`equiv`, `load`, `outIL`) represents the **reconstructed simplified circuit** used to compute the final load response.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing the R_th Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[Does the network contain only independent sources] -->|YES| pathA[Use Method A Source Deactivation]
    q1 -->|NO contains dependent sources| pathB[Use Method C Test Source Method]
    pathA --> r1[Set V sources to short and I sources to open then compute Req]
    pathB --> r2[Deactivate all INDEPENDENT sources only leave dependent sources intact]
    r2 --> r3[Apply V test equals 1V or I test equals 1A at terminals a b]
    r3 --> r4[Compute Rth equals V test divided by I test]
    pathA --> altPath[Alternative Method B Compute Voc and Isc then Rth equals Voc divided by Isc]
    altPath --> r1
```

> **Pitfall guard for students:** Many students incorrectly deactivate **dependent sources** as well. **The rule is: dependent sources MUST be left intact during the test source method (Method C), because their value depends on a circuit variable.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Thevenin's Theorem. Mention the two parameters that completely characterize the Thevenin equivalent circuit of a linear bilateral network.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution):**

> **Statement (2 marks):** Thevenin's Theorem states that *any linear, bilateral, active network containing voltage and current sources can be replaced, with respect to any pair of terminals, by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source V_th in series with a single resistance R_th.*

> **Two parameters (1 mark):** The two parameters are:
> 1. **Thevenin Equivalent Voltage (V_th)** — equal to the open-circuit voltage across the terminals.
> 2. **Thevenin Equivalent Resistance (R_th)** — the equivalent resistance looking back into the network with all independent sources deactivated.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why is Thevenin's theorem not applicable to non-linear networks? Give one example of a non-linear element.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution):**

> **Reason (2 marks):** Thevenin's theorem relies on the **superposition principle** and the assumption of **linearity** (proportional relationship between voltage and current). Non-linear elements do not obey Ohm's Law and their V-I characteristics are not straight lines, so a single Thevenin voltage and resistance cannot uniquely represent their behaviour across all operating points.

> **Example (1 mark):** A **semiconductor diode** (forward-biased) is a non-linear element — its V-I curve is exponential, not linear.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The KTU University Exam pattern allows an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** For the circuit shown below, find the Thevenin equivalent across terminals a-b using source transformation / nodal analysis. The circuit has a **20 V** source in series with a **4 Ω** resistor, this combination in parallel with a **2 A** current source, with a **6 Ω** resistor in series, leading to terminal a; terminal b is at the common ground. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A load resistance of **R_L = 12 Ω** is connected across terminals a-b of the Thevenin equivalent obtained in part (a). Calculate the load current, load voltage, and the power delivered to the load. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Network:**
- Left branch: 20 V source in series with 4 Ω resistor.
- Middle branch: 2 A current source.
- Right branch: 6 Ω resistor in series, ending at terminal a.
- Terminal b is grounded.

**Step 1: Remove R_L and Find V_oc (= V_th)**

Apply **source transformation** on the left branch: 20 V in series with 4 Ω → **5 A current source** in parallel with 4 Ω.

> **Logic:** A series V-R combination can always be transformed to a parallel I-R combination using $I = V/R = 20/4 = 5$ A.

After transformation, we have at the top node (let's call it node c):
- **5 A** current source (from transformation) — pushing current into node c.
- **2 A** current source (original) — also pushing current into node c (assume).
- **4 Ω** resistor in parallel.
- **6 Ω** resistor going to terminal a (open circuit, so no current flows through it when finding V_oc — wait, but 6 Ω connects node c to terminal a, and terminal a is open).

**Re-analysis using Nodal Analysis:**

Let $V_c$ = voltage at the intermediate node, and $V_a = V_{th}$ = voltage at terminal a.

Since terminal a is open, no current flows through the 6 Ω resistor. Therefore, by KVL, $V_c = V_a$ (no drop across 6 Ω).

**KCL at super-node a–c:**

Current entering from sources = Current leaving through resistors

$$5 + 2 = \frac{V_a}{4} + \frac{V_a}{0\ (\text{open})}$$

Wait — let me re-examine. If a is open, the 6 Ω has no current, so $V_a = V_c$. The 4 Ω carries the entire source current back to ground.

$$7 = \frac{V_a}{4}$$

$$V_{th} = V_a = 28\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Find R_th**

Deactivate sources:
- 20 V source → short circuit.
- 2 A current source → open circuit.

Looking into a-b: the 4 Ω is in parallel with the 6 Ω (since the shorted 20 V source now connects one end of the 4 Ω to ground, and the open 2 A source removes that branch).

$$R_{th} = \frac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega$$

**Thevenin Equivalent: V_th = 28 V, R_th = 2.4 Ω.**

##### **Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)**

Reconnect R_L = 12 Ω.

**Load Current:**

$$I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \frac{28}{2.4 + 12} = \frac{28}{14.4} = 1.944\ \text{A}$$

**Load Voltage:**

$$V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = 1.944 \times 12 = 23.33\ \text{V}$$

(Or via Voltage Divider: $V_L = 28 \times \frac{12}{14.4} = 23.33$ V — same answer ✓)

**Power Delivered to Load:**

$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = (1.944)^2 \times 12 = 3.78 \times 12 = 45.35\ \text{W}$$

(Or: $P_L = V_L \cdot I_L = 23.33 \times 1.944 = 45.35$ W ✓)

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to find the Thevenin equivalent of a linear network containing a **dependent (controlled) source**. Why is the usual "deactivate all sources" method insufficient in this case? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the network containing a Current-Controlled Voltage Source (CCVS) of value **2 I_x** (where I_x is the current through a **1 Ω** sensing resistor) in series with a **3 Ω** resistor and a **5 V** independent source, find the Thevenin equivalent across the output terminals using the **Test Source Method**. The network has 4 Ω in parallel with the CCVS branch. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks)**

> **Why the standard method fails (3 marks):** When a dependent source is present, the "deactivate all sources" rule **does not apply** to the dependent source. The dependent source's value is governed by a circuit variable (a voltage or current elsewhere in the network). If we short-circuit a dependent voltage source, we destroy the controlling relationship, leading to a **wrong R_th**. The independent sources, however, can still be deactivated.

> **Correct Procedure — Test Source Method (4 marks):**
> 1. Deactivate **only the independent sources** (V → short, I → open). Leave the dependent source **intact**.
> 2. Apply a **fictitious test voltage source V_test** (typically 1 V for simplicity) at terminals a-b.
> 3. Compute the **resulting test current I_test** flowing out of the test source into terminal a.
> 4. Thevenin resistance: $\mathbf{R_{th} = V_{test} / I_{test}}$.
>
> *(Alternative: apply test current source, compute test voltage, ratio gives R_th.)*

##### **Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- CCVS: V_dep = 2·I_x, where I_x is the current through a 1 Ω sensing resistor in the circuit.
- 5 V independent source in series with 3 Ω resistor.
- 4 Ω resistor in parallel with the CCVS branch.
- Find Thevenin equivalent across output terminals a-b.

**Step 1: Deactivate the 5 V Independent Source (Short Circuit). Leave the CCVS intact.**

After deactivation, the 3 Ω is connected from one node to the ground (because the 5 V is shorted). The 1 Ω sensing resistor is in series with the 3 Ω (in the sensing branch for I_x).

**Step 2: Apply V_test = 1 V at terminals a-b.**

Let $V_a = 1$ V (test voltage), and $V_b = 0$ V.

The current from the test source splits between the 4 Ω resistor branch and the CCVS branch.

**Step 3: Apply KCL at node a.**

The current entering node a from V_test equals the sum of currents leaving:

- Through 4 Ω: $I_1 = 1/4$ A.
- Through CCVS branch: The CCVS maintains 2·I_x, so the current through the 3 Ω and 1 Ω combination is determined by the loop.

**Determining I_x (current through 1 Ω sensing resistor):**

The CCVS branch loop equation: Going around the loop containing the 1 Ω, 3 Ω, and the CCVS:

$$V_a = I_x \cdot (1 + 3) + 2 I_x = 4 I_x + 2 I_x = 6 I_x$$

$$I_x = \frac{V_a}{6} = \frac{1}{6}\ \text{A}$$

**Current into CCVS branch (I_2):**

The current entering this branch from node a equals the current through the 1 Ω (since the 1 Ω is in series with the CCVS in the loop). So:

$$I_2 = I_x = \frac{1}{6}\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4: Total Test Current:**

$$I_{test} = I_1 + I_2 = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{3 + 2}{12} = \frac{5}{12}\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5: Thevenin Resistance:**

$$R_{th} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{1}{5/12} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4\ \Omega$$

> **Key Result:** R_th = 2.4 Ω, with V_th computable from a separate open-circuit analysis of the original network.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Dependent source deactivation error (–2 to –3 marks):** The single most common mistake in KTU exams is **shorting/open-circuiting the dependent source** along with the independent source. This gives a **spuriously large or negative R_th** and full credit is denied. **Remember: only independent sources are deactivated.**
>
> 2. **Polarity confusion in V_th (–1 mark):** Students often compute the **magnitude** of V_th correctly but assign the **wrong polarity** (e.g., + at b instead of + at a). This leads to a **reversed-direction load current** in the next step. Always verify by physical intuition: the terminal nearer to the positive source terminal should be marked '+'.
>
> 3. **Unit omission (–0.5 mark):** KTU valuators are strict about units. Always write V, A, Ω, W — never leave numerical answers unitless.
>
> 4. **Confusion between V_oc/I_sc method and test-source method (–2 marks):** The V_oc/I_sc method (Method B) works for **dependent sources too**, but students often mix it up with the test source method (Method C) and apply Method A (pure deactivation) by mistake.
>
> 5. **Algebraic error in nodal analysis (–1 to –2 marks):** KCL sign errors at the node are very common. **Always draw current arrows leaving the node and sum them to zero.** Double-check signs after writing the equation.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Thevenin's Theorem** reduces any linear bilateral network to a **single V_th in series with R_th** as seen from a specific pair of terminals.
- [ ] **V_th = V_oc** — the open-circuit voltage across the load terminals (load removed).
- [ ] **R_th** can be found by **three** methods:
  - Method A: Deactivate **independent** sources only.
  - Method B: $R_{th} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$.
  - Method C: Test source method ($V_{test}/I_{test}$) — **mandatory** for dependent sources.
- [ ] **Dependent sources are NEVER deactivated** when computing R_th.
- [ ] After Thevenin reduction, the load current is simply $I_L = V_{th}/(R_{th} + R_L)$ — a one-step Ohm's Law.
- [ ] **Thevenin equivalent is unique** for a given terminal pair.
- [ ] The theorem is **invalid for non-linear** elements (diodes, saturated magnetics, etc.).
- [ ] **Maximum Power Transfer** ($P_{max} = V_{th}^2 / 4 R_{th}$ at $R_L = R_{th}$) follows directly from Thevenin reduction.
- [ ] Always **label the polarity** of V_th (positive at the terminal with higher potential under open-circuit condition).
- [ ] Always **include units** in final answers (V, A, Ω, W).
- [ ] KTU-favoured techniques for V_th: **Nodal Analysis** is usually fastest; **Mesh Analysis** works for planar circuits; **Source Transformation** is elegant for ladder networks.
- [ ] **Sign convention in KCL:** Write $\sum I_{leaving} = 0$ for nodal analysis — this is the most foolproof approach for the open-circuit voltage computation.
- [ ] In the exam, **draw the Thevenin equivalent circuit explicitly** as a separate sub-figure — it earns you 1–2 presentation marks.
- [ ] **Cross-verify** your R_th using Method B ($V_{oc}/I_{sc}$) as a sanity check — if both methods agree, the answer is almost certainly correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocity Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reciprocity Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Theorem (KTU Board Standard Definition)**
> *"In any linear, bilateral, time-invariant network consisting of passive elements (R, L, C), containing a **single independent source**, the ratio of **excitation** (source) to **response** (measured quantity) remains constant when the positions of the excitation and the response are interchanged."*

Mathematically, if a voltage source $V_s$ applied in branch $AB$ produces a current $I$ in branch $CD$, then transferring the **same** source $V_s$ to branch $CD$ will produce the **same** current $I$ in branch $AB$.

## 1.2 Two Canonical Forms of the Theorem

| Form | Excitation (Source) | Response (Measured) | Reciprocal Response |
|:----:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| **Form I (Voltage–Current)** | Voltage Source $V_s$ | Current $I$ | Same current $I$ at swapped terminals |
| **Form II (Current–Voltage)** | Current Source $I_s$ | Voltage $V$ | Same voltage $V$ at swapped terminals |

## 1.3 Conditions of Validity (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-conditions that MUST hold for Reciprocity to apply:**
> 1. The network must be **linear** — obeys Ohm's Law, superposition holds.
> 2. The network must be **bilateral** — element behaviour identical in both directions.
> 3. The network must be **time-invariant** — parameters do not change with time.
> 4. Only **one independent source** must be present at a time.
> 5. **Initial conditions** of all energy storage elements (L, C) must be **zero**.
> 6. **Dependent sources** (if any) must not be controlled by variables in the branch being transferred.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The Water Pipe Echo

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a network of interconnected water pipes (the passive resistances). If you push water (voltage source) into pipe A and measure the flow rate (current) coming out of pipe B, you will get the **exact same flow rate** if you instead push the same water pressure into pipe B and measure the output at pipe A. The pipe network "doesn't care" which end you push from — the transmission property is symmetric. This symmetry is the heart of **Reciprocity**.

## 1.5 Where This Theorem Shines in Engineering

- **Antenna theory**: Transmitting and receiving properties of an antenna are reciprocal.
- **Two-port network analysis**: Forms the foundation of the symmetry property $z_{12} = z_{21}$.
- **Power systems**: Used in symmetrical fault analysis and load-transfer studies.
- **Filter design**: Simplifies measurement of transfer functions.
- **Communication circuits**: Justifies the swap of transmitter and receiver paths.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Theorem

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Identify the source branch (excitation port)** — branch AB where the independent source is originally connected.
2. **Identify the response branch (measurement port)** — branch CD where the resulting current/voltage is measured.
3. **Compute the response ratio** $\dfrac{\text{Response}}{\text{Excitation}}$ under the original configuration.
4. **Interchange the source and response positions** — move source to branch CD, place an ammeter/voltmeter at branch AB.
5. **Compute the new response ratio** — by the theorem, it equals the original ratio.
6. **Conclude** that the network behaves identically in both directions, confirming symmetry.

## 2.2 Proof Strategy — Mesh Analysis Approach

The proof uses the **determinant of the mesh impedance matrix**. Because the network contains only bilateral elements, the matrix is **symmetric**, which directly yields reciprocity.

For an $n$-mesh network with a single voltage source $V_s$ in mesh $i$:

$$\mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I} = \mathbf{V}$$

Solving for the current in mesh $k$ using **Cramer's Rule**:

$$I_k = \frac{\Delta_{ik} \cdot V_s}{\Delta}$$

When the source is moved to mesh $k$ and current measured in mesh $i$:

$$I_i' = \frac{\Delta_{ki} \cdot V_s}{\Delta}$$

Because $\mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric (bilateral network), the cofactors are equal: $\Delta_{ik} = \Delta_{ki}$, therefore:

$$I_k = I_i'$$

This is the algebraic essence of reciprocity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Description | Conditions |
|:-:|:------------------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| 1 | $V_{s,AB} / I_{CD} = V_{s,CD} / I_{AB}$ | Form I: Voltage–Current Reciprocity | Linear, bilateral, single source |
| 2 | $I_{s,AB} \cdot V_{CD} = I_{s,CD} \cdot V_{AB}$ | Form II: Current–Voltage Reciprocity | Same as above |
| 3 | $\Delta_{ik} = \Delta_{ki}$ | Symmetric cofactors of mesh impedance matrix | Bilateral network |
| 4 | $I_k = I_k'$ | Current at port = Current at port (after swap) | Reciprocity result |
| 5 | $z_{12} = z_{21}$ | Two-port Z-parameter reciprocity | For passive bilateral networks |
| 6 | $y_{12} = y_{21}$ | Two-port Y-parameter reciprocity | For passive bilateral networks |
| 7 | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | Hybrid-parameter reciprocity (signed) | For passive bilateral networks |
| 8 | $A \cdot D - B \cdot C = 1$ | ABCD-parameter reciprocity | For passive bilateral networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to remember:** $V$ in **Volts (V)**, $I$ in **Amperes (A)**, $R$ in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, $L$ in **Henries (H)**, $C$ in **Farads (F)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Engineering**: The Friis transmission equation relies on antenna reciprocity — gain in transmit mode equals gain in receive mode.
- **Network Analysers**: Reciprocity allows measurement in only one direction to characterise the entire two-port.
- **Power System Protection**: Fault current at any bus can be computed by placing the source at that bus instead — saving simulation time.
- **Crystal & Microwave Circuits**: Simplifies S-parameter extraction since $S_{12} = S_{21}$ for reciprocal media.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Proof of the Reciprocity Theorem (Mesh Analysis)

### Setup
Consider a linear, bilateral, passive network with **$n$ independent meshes**. A single voltage source $V_s$ is placed in **mesh $i$** and we measure the resulting current $I_k$ in **mesh $k$**.

### Step 1: Write the Mesh Equation Matrix

For a linear bilateral network, the mesh impedance matrix $\mathbf{Z}$ is real, symmetric, and square ($n \times n$). The mesh equation in matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I} &= \mathbf{V} \\
\begin{bmatrix}
Z_{11} & Z_{12} & \cdots & Z_{1n} \\
Z_{21} & Z_{22} & \cdots & Z_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
Z_{n1} & Z_{n2} & \cdots & Z_{nn}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_n
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ \vdots \\ V_s \\ \vdots \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

The vector $\mathbf{V}$ has $V_s$ only at the $i^{th}$ row (since the source exists solely in mesh $i$); all other rows are zero.

### Step 2: Apply Cramer's Rule to Solve for $I_k$

By Cramer's rule, the current in mesh $k$ is:

$$I_k = \frac{\Delta_k}{\Delta}$$

where $\Delta = \det(\mathbf{Z})$ and $\Delta_k$ is the determinant obtained by replacing the $k^{th}$ column of $\mathbf{Z}$ with $\mathbf{V}$.

Since only the $i^{th}$ entry of $\mathbf{V}$ is non-zero, $\Delta_k$ collapses to $V_s$ times the cofactor $\Delta_{ik}$:

$$I_k = \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ik}}{\Delta}$$

### Step 3: Swap the Source and Measurement Positions

Now, move the voltage source $V_s$ to **mesh $k$** and measure the current in **mesh $i$**. The new excitation vector has $V_s$ at the $k^{th}$ row only. The current in mesh $i$ becomes:

$$I_i' = \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ki}}{\Delta}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Symmetry of Bilateral Networks

For a **bilateral** network, the impedance matrix is **symmetric**, meaning $Z_{pq} = Z_{qp}$ for all $p, q$. A fundamental property of determinants of symmetric matrices is that cofactors are also symmetric:

$$\Delta_{ik} = \Delta_{ki}$$

### Step 5: Conclude the Theorem

Substituting the equality of cofactors:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_k &= \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ik}}{\Delta} \\
I_i' &= \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ki}}{\Delta} = \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ik}}{\Delta} \\
\therefore \quad I_k &= I_i'
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Q.E.D. — Reciprocity Theorem proven:** The current at the response port remains identical whether the source is at the original excitation port or at the response port.

## 3.2 Numerical Example Worked-Out (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In the network shown below, a 10 V source in branch AB produces 2 A in branch CD. Verify reciprocity.

### Step 1: Original Configuration
$V_{AB} = 10\text{ V}$, $I_{CD} = 2\text{ A}$.

$$\text{Transfer ratio} = \frac{I_{CD}}{V_{AB}} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 \text{ A/V}$$

### Step 2: Reciprocal Configuration
Move the 10 V source to branch CD and place the ammeter at AB. By reciprocity:

$$I_{AB} = 0.2 \times 10 = 2 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3: Verification of Ratio Symmetry

$$\frac{I_{AB}}{V_{CD}} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 \text{ A/V} \quad \checkmark$$

The transfer ratio is identical in both directions.

## 3.3 Two-Port Parameter Reciprocity — Derivations

For a passive bilateral two-port network, all four parameter sets obey reciprocity:

### Z-Parameters
$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= z_{11} I_1 + z_{12} I_2 \\
V_2 &= z_{21} I_1 + z_{22} I_2
\end{aligned}$$

By reciprocity: $\boxed{z_{12} = z_{21}}$

### Y-Parameters
$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= y_{11} V_1 + y_{12} V_2 \\
I_2 &= y_{21} V_1 + y_{22} V_2
\end{aligned}$$

By reciprocity: $\boxed{y_{12} = y_{21}}$

### ABCD (Transmission) Parameters
$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A V_2 - B I_2 \\
I_1 &= C V_2 - D I_2
\end{aligned}$$

By reciprocity: $\boxed{AD - BC = 1}$

### h-Parameters
$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \\
I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2
\end{aligned}$$

By reciprocity: $\boxed{h_{12} = -h_{21}}$ *(note the negative sign, characteristic of hybrid parameters)*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Reciprocity Theorem Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Original Network Configuration"] --> nodeB["Voltage Source Vs in Branch AB"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Ammeter Measures Current I in Branch CD"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute Transfer Ratio I divided by Vs"]

    nodeE["Reciprocal Network Configuration"] --> nodeF["Voltage Source Vs Moved to Branch CD"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Ammeter Measures Current I in Branch AB"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Compute New Transfer Ratio I divided by Vs"]

    nodeD --> nodeI["Reciprocity Holds if Both Ratios are Equal"]
    nodeH --> nodeI

    nodeI --> nodeJ["Conclusion Linear Bilateral Network is Symmetric"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Proof Architecture (Mesh Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Start Linear Bilateral n mesh Network"] --> node2["Form Mesh Impedance Matrix Z"]
    node2 --> node3["Matrix Z is Symmetric Zpq equals Zqp"]
    node3 --> node4["Place Source Vs in Mesh i"]
    node4 --> node5["Solve Ik using Cramer Rule Ik equals Vs times Delta ik divided by Delta"]
    node5 --> node6["Move Source to Mesh k"]
    node6 --> node7["Solve I prime i using Cramer Rule I prime i equals Vs times Delta ki divided by Delta"]
    node7 --> node8["Apply Symmetry Delta ik equals Delta ki"]
    node8 --> node9["Conclude Ik equals I prime i QED Reciprocity Proven"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Two-Port Reciprocity Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PORT1["Port 1 Original"]
        dir1["V1 Applied"] --> res1["Network N"]
        res1 --> cur1["I1 Measured"]
    end

    subgraph PORT2["Port 2 Reciprocal"]
        dir2["V1 Moved to Port 2"] --> res2["Same Network N"]
        res2 --> cur2["I1 Measured at Port 1"]
    end

    PORT1 -.->|"Equal Transfer Ratio"| PORT2
```

## 4.4 Detailed Numerical Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source Vs equals 10V"] --> brA["Branch AB"]
    brA --> R1["Resistor R1 equals 2 ohm"]
    R1 --> mid1["Node M"]

    mid1 --> R2["Resistor R2 equals 4 ohm"]
    R2 --> brC["Branch CD"]
    brC --> measA["Ammeter I measured"]

    mid1 --> R3["Resistor R3 equals 6 ohm"]
    R3 --> brD["Branch CD parallel path"]
    brD --> measA

    measA --> retA["Return Node"]

    srcA -.->|"After Reciprocity Swap"| srcB["Source Vs equals 10V in Branch CD"]
    srcB --> measB["Ammeter in Branch AB"]
    measB --> retB["Return Node same value I"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Reciprocity Theorem. List the conditions for its validity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** In a linear, bilateral, time-invariant single-source network, the ratio of excitation to response remains unchanged when the source and the point of measurement are interchanged.

**Conditions (any three for full marks):**
1. Network must be **linear** (Ohm's Law valid).
2. Network must be **bilateral** (symmetric elements).
3. **Single independent source** only.
4. **Zero initial conditions** in L and C.
5. **Time-invariant** parameters.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Reciprocity Theorem and Superposition Theorem in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Reciprocity Theorem | Superposition Theorem |
|:-------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| Number of Sources | **One** source at a time | **Multiple** sources allowed |
| Operation | Interchanges source and response positions | Superposes responses of each source |
| Validity | Linear + bilateral networks | Linear networks (bilateral not required) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State and prove the Reciprocity Theorem for a linear bilateral network using mesh analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the network shown in **Figure Q-A(b)**, a 12 V source placed in branch 1-1' produces 3 A in branch 2-2'. Using the Reciprocity Theorem, find:
   (i) The current in branch 1-1' if the 12 V source is moved to branch 2-2'.
   (ii) The voltage across branch 2-2' if a 4 A current source is placed in branch 1-1'. **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Statement and Proof (7 Marks)

**Statement:** Refer to Section 1.1 for the formal definition. *(1 mark)*

**Proof:** Consider an $n$-mesh bilateral linear network. *(3 marks for proof logic — see Section 3.1)*

The mesh impedance matrix is symmetric: $Z_{pq} = Z_{qp}$. By Cramer's rule:

$$I_k = \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ik}}{\Delta} \quad \text{and} \quad I_i' = \frac{V_s \cdot \Delta_{ki}}{\Delta}$$

Since $\Delta_{ik} = \Delta_{ki}$ for a symmetric matrix, we obtain $I_k = I_i'$. **Hence the theorem is proved.** *(3 marks for concluding and stating reciprocity condition)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V_s = 12\text{ V}$ in branch 1-1', produces $I = 3\text{ A}$ in branch 2-2'.

**(i) Current in branch 1-1' when source moves to branch 2-2':**

By reciprocity, the transfer ratio is preserved:

$$\frac{I_{1-1'}}{V_{2-2'}} = \frac{I_{2-2'}}{V_{1-1'}} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25 \text{ A/V}$$

$$\boxed{I_{1-1'} = 0.25 \times 12 = 3 \text{ A}}$$

*(3 marks for calculation)*

**(ii) Voltage across branch 2-2' for a 4 A current source in branch 1-1':**

Using Form II of Reciprocity (Current–Voltage form):

$$\frac{V_{2-2'}}{I_{s,1-1'}} = \text{Transfer resistance} = \frac{V_{s,1-1'}}{I_{2-2'}} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{V_{2-2'} = 4 \text{ }\Omega \times 4 \text{ A} = 16 \text{ V}}$$

*(4 marks for calculation including transfer resistance concept)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the reciprocity condition in terms of **two-port Z-parameters** and **Y-parameters** for a passive bilateral network. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In the circuit of **Figure Q-B(b)**, $R_1 = 5\text{ }\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ }\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\text{ }\Omega$ connected in a T-network. A 20 V source applied at port 1 produces a current of 1 A in the branch connecting to port 2. Determine:
   (i) The current at port 1 if the 20 V source is moved to port 2.
   (ii) Verify using mesh analysis whether reciprocity holds. **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Two-Parameter Reciprocity (7 Marks)

**Z-Parameters Reciprocity:** *(3 marks)*

For a passive bilateral two-port:

$$z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0} = z_{21}$$

This is mathematically identical to the Reciprocity Theorem statement: voltage at port 1 due to a current injected at port 2 equals voltage at port 2 due to the same current injected at port 1.

**Y-Parameters Reciprocity:** *(4 marks)*

From the Z-Y conversion: $y_{12} = -\dfrac{z_{12}}{\det(\mathbf{Z})} = -\dfrac{z_{21}}{\det(\mathbf{Z})} = y_{21}$

$$\boxed{y_{12} = y_{21}}$$

Since the determinant of the Z-matrix is non-zero for a passive network, both parameter sets confirm reciprocity.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Verification (7 Marks)

**Given:** T-network with $R_1 = 5\text{ }\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ }\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\text{ }\Omega$.

The T-network mesh equations when source $V_s = 20\text{ V}$ at port 1, port 2 open:

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1 + R_2) I_1 - R_2 I_2 &= V_s \\
-R_2 I_1 + (R_2 + R_3) I_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
15 I_1 - 10 I_2 &= 20 \\
-10 I_1 + 30 I_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

From the second equation: $I_1 = 3 I_2$, so $I_2 = I_1 / 3$.

*(3 marks — setting up and solving mesh equations)*

**(i) Current at port 1 when source moves to port 2:**

By reciprocity, $I_{port1,\text{reciprocal}} = I_{port2,\text{original}}$.

We need $I_2$ (which is the current entering port 2). Since port 2 is open in the original setup, the current through $R_3$ branch is $I_2$.

From Cramer's rule:

$$\Delta = (15)(30) - (-10)(-10) = 450 - 100 = 350$$

$$I_2 = \frac{15 \times 20 - 0}{350} = \frac{300}{350} = \frac{6}{7} \text{ A} \approx 0.857 \text{ A}$$

By reciprocity: $I_1' = I_2 = \dfrac{6}{7} \text{ A} \approx 0.857 \text{ A}$

$$\boxed{I_{port1,\text{reciprocal}} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.857 \text{ A}}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Verification:**

When the source is moved to port 2 and we measure $I_1'$, the mesh equations become symmetric (swap source to mesh 2, measure in mesh 1). By the symmetry of the determinant, the result must be identical, confirming reciprocity. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reciprocity Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify conditions**: If the network contains dependent sources, reciprocity **fails** — students often lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Sign convention error**: When using Form II (current source), the polarity of the measured voltage must follow the assumed reference direction — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for sign mismatches.
