<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain why the current through an inductor and the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The current through an inductor is given by $v_L = L\,di_L/dt$. For the current to change instantaneously, $di/dt$ would have to be infinite, requiring an **infinite voltage** across the inductor — which is physically impossible in any practical circuit with finite source voltages. Hence, $i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+)$.

Similarly, the voltage across a capacitor is governed by $i_C = C\,dv_C/dt$. For instantaneous voltage change, $dv/dt \to \infty$ would require an **infinite current** — physically unrealizable. Hence, $v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+)$.

These continuity laws arise from the fact that energy stored in inductors ($W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$) and capacitors ($W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$) **cannot change abruptly**, as that would imply infinite power.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the equivalent circuits of an inductor and capacitor at $t = 0^+$, $t = 0^-$, and $t \to \infty$ for DC excitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Instant | Inductor $L$ | Capacitor $C$ |
|---|---|---|
| $t = 0^-$ | Short circuit (if $i_L(0^-)=0$); else current source $i_L(0^-)$ | Open circuit (if $v_C(0^-)=0$); else voltage source $v_C(0^-)$ |
| $t = 0^+$ | Current source $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$ | Voltage source $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$ |
| $t \to \infty$ | Short circuit ($v_L = 0$) | Open circuit ($i_C = 0$) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**A series RL circuit with $R = 10\;\Omega$ and $L = 5\;\text{H}$ is connected to a $50\;\text{V}$ DC source through a switch at $t = 0$. Find:**
**(a)** The initial and final values of inductor current $i_L(t)$.
**(b)** The expression for $i_L(t)$ and the time at which $i_L$ reaches $80\%$ of its final value.

#### Part (a) — Initial and Final Values [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Initial Condition ($t = 0^-$):**

Before the switch closes, no current flows in the circuit:

$$i_L(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Continuity at $t = 0^+$:**

$$i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-) = 0\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Final Condition ($t \to \infty$):**

In DC steady state, the inductor acts as a short circuit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L(\infty) &= \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{50}{10} = 5\;\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary:**

$$\boxed{i_L(0) = 0\;\text{A}, \quad i_L(\infty) = 5\;\text{A}}$$

#### Part (b) — Transient Expression and 80% Time [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — General Solution:**

For a first-order RL circuit with step input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_L(t) &= i_L(\infty) + \left[i_L(0) - i_L(\infty)\right] e^{-t/\tau} \\[6pt]
i_L(t) &= 5 + (0 - 5)e^{-t/0.5} \\[6pt]
i_L(t) &= 5\left(1 - e^{-2t}\right)\;\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to Reach 80% of Final Value:**

Set $i_L(t_{80}) = 0.8 \times 5 = 4\;\text{A}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5\left(1 - e^{-2t_{80}}\right) &= 4 \\[4pt]
1 - e^{-2t_{80}} &= 0.8 \\[4pt]
e^{-2t_{80}} &= 0.2 \\[4pt]
-2t_{80} &= \ln(0.2) = -1.6094 \\[4pt]
t_{80} &= \frac{1.6094}{2} \approx 0.805\;\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{i_L(t) = 5(1 - e^{-2t})\;\text{A}, \quad t_{80\%} \approx 0.805\;\text{s}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**A capacitor $C = 100\;\mu\text{F}$ is initially charged to $50\;\text{V}$. At $t = 0$, it is connected across a resistor $R = 200\;\Omega$. Determine:**
**(a)** The initial and final values of capacitor voltage and current.
**(b)** The expression for $v_C(t)$ and the energy dissipated in $R$ over $(0, \infty)$.

#### Part (a) — Initial and Final Values [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Initial Conditions ($t = 0^-$):**

The capacitor is pre-charged with no source connection:

$$v_C(0^-) = 50\;\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Continuity at $t = 0^+$:**

$$v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-) = 50\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Initial Current:**

Applying KVL around the loop (only $R$ and $C$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_C(0^+) &= \frac{v_C(0^+)}{R} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25\;\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Conditions ($t \to \infty$):**

Capacitor discharges fully; acts as open circuit:

$$v_C(\infty) = 0\;\text{V}, \quad i_C(\infty) = 0\;\text{A}$$

**Summary:**

$$\boxed{v_C(0) = 50\;\text{V},\quad i_C(0) = 0.25\;\text{A},\quad v_C(\infty) = 0,\quad i_C(\infty) = 0}$$

#### Part (b) — Transient Expression and Energy Dissipated [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau = RC = (200)(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.02\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_C(t) &= v_C(\infty) + \left[v_C(0) - v_C(\infty)\right] e^{-t/\tau} \\[6pt]
v_C(t) &= 0 + (50 - 0)e^{-t/0.02} \\[6pt]
\boxed{v_C(t) &= 50\,e^{-50t}\;\text{V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Current Expression:**

$$
i_C(t) = 0.25\,e^{-50t}\;\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy Dissipated in $R$:**

By conservation of energy, all energy initially stored in the capacitor must be dissipated in $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_R &= W_C(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}C\,v_C^2(0) \\[4pt]
W_R &= \tfrac{1}{2}(100 \times 10^{-6})(50)^2 \\[4pt]
W_R &= \tfrac{1}{2}(100 \times 10^{-6})(2500) \\[4pt]
\boxed{W_R &= 0.125\;\text{J} = 125\;\text{mJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification by Integration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_R &= \int_{0}^{\infty} i_C^2(t)\, R\, dt \\[4pt]
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} (0.25)^2 e^{-100t} \cdot 200\, dt \\[4pt]
&= (0.0625)(200)\left[\frac{-e^{-100t}}{100}\right]_0^\infty \\[4pt]
&= 12.5 \times \frac{1}{100} = 0.125\;\text{J} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting continuity laws** — writing $v_C(0^+) = 0$ without justification when the capacitor is pre-charged. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Wrong polarity of initial capacitor voltage** — if the reference direction in the diagram is opposite to the actual charge polarity, the initial voltage is **negative**. Always check arrows! **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Replacing an inductor with a short circuit at $t = 0^+$** — WRONG! At $t = 0^+$, use a **current source** $i_L(0)$, not a short. Only at $t = \infty$ does it become a short. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Using wrong time constant** — for Norton-equivalent resistance seen by the capacitor/inductor. Always compute $R_{Th}$ or $R_N$ **after** deactivating sources. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing $\mu\text{F}$ with Farads without conversion. **Penalty: 0.5 mark.**
>
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always draw THREE separate circuits: $t = 0^-$, $t = 0^+$, and $t = \infty$. Mark all computed values clearly on the diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Initial & Final Conditions**

- ☐ **Continuity Laws:** $v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+)$ and $i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+)$ — memorize first.
- ☐ **Capacitor current CAN jump**, inductor voltage CAN jump — but not the stored quantities.
- ☐ **At $t = 0^-$ and $t \to \infty$**, replace $L$ with short and $C$ with open (DC steady state).
- ☐ **At $t = 0^+$**, replace $L$ with current source $i_L(0)$ and $C$ with voltage source $v_C(0)$.
- ☐ **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ for RC circuits; $\tau = L/R$ for RL circuits.
- ☐ **General first-order solution:** $x(t) = x(\infty) + [x(0) - x(\infty)]e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ☐ **Energy stored:** $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$, $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ — dissipated as heat in resistors.
- ☐ **Always draw three equivalent circuits** — examiners reward clear working.
- ☐ **Watch for pre-charged capacitors** — initial conditions are not zero.
- ☐ **Sign conventions** — verify polarity of $v_C$ and direction of $i_L$ against assumed reference.
- ☐ **Final value theorem check:** $x(\infty)$ should be a DC steady-state solution (no derivatives).
- ☐ **KTU weightage:** Expect one full 14-mark question per exam on this topic.

---
**END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: INITIAL AND FINAL CONDITIONS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Laplace Transforms and Inverse Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Review of Laplace Transforms and Inverse Laplace Transforms # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Laplace Transform** is a powerful integral transform that converts a continuous time-domain function $f(t)$, defined for $t \geq 0$, into a complex frequency-domain (s-domain) function $F(s)$. It is the single most indispensable mathematical tool in the KTU 2024 electrical network syllabus for solving linear, time-invariant (LTI) differential equations that describe RLC circuits. The **unilateral (one-sided) Laplace Transform** is formally defined as: $$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f(t)\, dt$$ where: - $f(t)$ is the time-domain function (causal, $t \geq 0$). - $s$ is the complex frequency variable, $s = \sigma + j\omega$, where $\sigma$ and $\omega$ are real numbers. - $F(s)$ is the transformed (s-domain) function. - The integral converges only when $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_c$, where $\sigma_c$ is the **abscissa of convergence**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The unilateral Laplace Transform is *the* standard tool because it inherently incorporates **initial conditions** of energy-storage elements (inductors and capacitors) into the s-domain model. This is why it is preferred over the Fourier Transform for transient circuit analysis. The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is the recovery operation: $$f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds$$ For engineering applications, we never evaluate this complex integral directly. Instead, we use algebraic techniques — primarily **partial fraction expansion** combined with standard transform tables. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — The "Simultaneous Translator":** > Imagine you are watching a movie in **French** (time domain) but you only understand **English** (frequency/algebraic domain). The Laplace Transform is like a **real-time simultaneous translator** sitting beside you. It listens to the French dialogue and instantly writes the English subtitles. > > In circuit analysis, the time domain is filled with messy **differential equations** (hard to solve). The Laplace Transform "translates" these into simple **algebraic equations** in $s$ (very easy to solve). Once solved, the **Inverse Laplace Transform** "translates back" the algebraic answer into a clean time-domain response $f(t)$ — like current $i(t)$ or voltage $v(t)$. ## 1.3 Why Laplace Transforms are Essential in Electrical Networks - **Handling Initial Conditions Automatically:** Unlike classical differential equation methods, where $i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^-)$ must be separately plugged in, the s-domain equivalent circuits absorb initial conditions as independent sources. - **Unified Treatment of Steady-State and Transients:** Both DC steady-state and transient responses are obtained in a single algebraic sweep. - **Convolution Becomes Multiplication:** Complex time-domain convolutions (used in system response) reduce to simple products $Y(s) = H(s) \cdot X(s)$. - **Direct Pole-Zero Analysis:** The roots of the denominator of $F(s)$ (the *poles*) directly dictate the **natural response modes** of the network. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Parameters | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $s$ | Complex frequency | rad/s (or nepers/s for real part) | | $\sigma_c$ | Abscissa of convergence | Real number | | $\omega$ | Angular frequency | rad/s | | $t$ | Time | seconds (s) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Existence of the Laplace Transform For the integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f(t)\, dt$ to converge, $f(t)$ must satisfy: 1. **Piecewise Continuity:** $f(t)$ has at most a finite number of finite discontinuities in any finite interval. 2. **Exponential Order:** There exist real constants $M > 0$, $\sigma_c$, and $T > 0$ such that $\vert f(t) \vert \leq M e^{\sigma_c t}$ for all $t \geq T$. If these conditions hold, $F(s)$ exists for all $s$ where $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_c$. This region is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**. ## 2.2 Linearity Property The Laplace Transform is a **linear operator**. For any constants $a$ and $b$: $$\mathcal{L}\{a f_1(t) \pm b f_2(t)\} = a F_1(s) \pm b F_2(s)$$ > **Why it matters:** Allows decomposition of complex signals (e.g., a damped sinusoid $e^{-at}\sin\omega t$) into sums of elementary signals whose transforms are tabulated. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Properties Each property below is a high-yield KTU examination item. The "Why" column explains its circuit interpretation. | Property | Time Domain | s-Domain | Why / How it Helps | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Linearity** | $a f_1(t) \pm b f_2(t)$ | $a F_1(s) \pm b F_2(s)$ | Splits complex waveforms into tabulated pieces. | | **Time Shifting** | $f(t - a) u(t-a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ | Models **delayed switching** in circuits. | | **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{at} f(t)$ | $F(s - a)$ | Handles **damped oscillations** ($e^{-at}\sin\omega t$). | | **Time Scaling** | $f(at)$ | $\frac{1}{a} F\!\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)$ | Compresses/expands time axes. | | **Differentiation** | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $s F(s) - f(0^-)$ | **Converts derivatives to algebra.** | | **nth Differentiation** | $\dfrac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}$ | $s^n F(s) - \sum_{k=1}^{n} s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0^-)$ | Handles higher-order ODEs. | | **Integration** | $\displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | Converts integral equations to algebra. | | **Multiplication by $t$** | $t f(t)$ | $-\dfrac{d F(s)}{ds}$ | Used for $t u(t)$, $t e^{-at}$ etc. | | **Division by $t$** | $\dfrac{f(t)}{t}$ | $\displaystyle\int_s^{\infty} F(u)\, du$ | Less common in KTU circuits. | | **Periodic Function** | $f(t)$ with period $T$ | $\dfrac{F_1(s)}{1 - e^{-Ts}}$ | Half-wave and full-wave rectifier outputs. | | **Initial Value** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ | Computes $i(0^+)$, $v(0^+)$ **without inverse transform**. | | **Final Value** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ | Computes steady-state value **directly**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Value Theorem Caveat (KTU Pitfall):** The Final Value Theorem is **valid only if all poles of $s F(s)$ lie strictly in the open left-half s-plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ for every pole). If any pole is on the $j\omega$-axis or in the right-half plane, the limit does not exist. ## 2.4 Convolution Theorem The convolution of two time-domain functions is defined as: $$f_1(t) * f_2(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f_1(\tau) f_2(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ The **Convolution Theorem** states: $$\mathcal{L}\{f_1(t) * f_2(t)\} = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s)$$ > **Real-world utility:** In network analysis, the output $Y(s)$ of any LTI system is the product of the **transfer function** $H(s)$ and the input $X(s)$. The time-domain response is the convolution of the **impulse response** $h(t)$ with the input $x(t)$ — this is the foundation of linear systems theory. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Standard Laplace Transform Pairs | $f(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ | $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$ | ROC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\delta(t)$ (unit impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ | | $u(t)$ (unit step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | | $t$ (ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | | $t^n$ (n an integer) | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | | $e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s + a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | | $t e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{1}{(s + a)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | | $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | | $\cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | | $e^{-at} \sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s + a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | | $e^{-at} \cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s + a}{(s + a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | | $\sinh(at)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ | | $\cosh(at)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Laplace Transform of the Unit Step Function $u(t)$ **Definition:** $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$, and $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. **Step 1:** Apply the definition. $$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} \cdot 1\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Pull out the constant and integrate. $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the limits. As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-st} \to 0$ (provided $\text{Re}(s) > 0$). At $t = 0$, $e^{0} = 1$. $$= \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-s} \right) = \frac{1}{s}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > 0}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Laplace Transform of the Exponential $e^{-at}$ **Step 1:** Apply the definition. $$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} e^{-at}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Combine exponents. $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the bounds (converges when $\text{Re}(s) > -a$). $$= \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-(s+a)} \right) = \frac{1}{s + a}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \frac{1}{s + a}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > -a}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laplace Transform of $\sin(\omega t)$ **Step 1:** Express sine using Euler's formula. $$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$ **Step 2:** Use linearity. $$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \mathcal{L}\{e^{j\omega t}\} - \mathcal{L}\{e^{-j\omega t}\} \right]$$ **Step 3:** Apply the exponential transform with $a = \mp j\omega$. $$= \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$ **Step 4:** Combine the fractions over a common denominator. $$= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$ **Step 5:** The $2j$ terms cancel. $$= \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Laplace Transform of the First Derivative (Differentiation Theorem) **Step 1:** Apply the definition to $f'(t)$. $$\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f'(t)\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Use integration by parts: $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$. Let $u = e^{-st}$ and $dv = f'(t)\, dt$. Then $du = -s e^{-st} dt$ and $v = f(t)$. $$= \left[ e^{-st} f(t) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) (-s e^{-st})\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-st} f(t) \to 0$ (assuming exponential order). At $t = 0$, the term is $-f(0^-)$. $$= 0 - f(0^-) + s \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f(t)\, dt$$ **Step 4:** The remaining integral is exactly $F(s)$. $$\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Example — Inverse Laplace via Partial Fractions **Problem:** Find $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{s + 3}{(s+1)(s+2)}\right\}$. **Step 1: Partial Fraction Decomposition.** $$\frac{s + 3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$ **Step 2: Multiply both sides by $(s+1)(s+2)$.** $$s + 3 = A(s+2) + B(s+1)$$ **Step 3: Solve for $A$ by setting $s = -1$ (cover-up rule).** $$-1 + 3 = A(-1 + 2) \;\Rightarrow\; A = 2$$ **Step 4: Solve for $B$ by setting $s = -2$.** $$-2 + 3 = B(-2 + 1) \;\Rightarrow\; B = -1$$ **Step 5: Rewrite the decomposed form.** $$F(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$ **Step 6: Apply the inverse Laplace transform term-by-term.** $$f(t) = 2 e^{-t} u(t) - e^{-2t} u(t)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{f(t) = \left(2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)}$$ ## 3.6 Worked Example — Convolution of $e^{-t}$ and $\sin(t)$ **Problem:** Evaluate $y(t) = e^{-t} u(t) * \sin(t) u(t)$. **Step 1:** Find individual transforms. $$F_1(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}, \qquad F_2(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 1}$$ **Step 2:** Multiply in s-domain. $$Y(s) = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s^2 + 1)}$$ **Step 3:** Partial fraction expansion. $$\frac{1}{(s+1)(s^2+1)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{Bs + C}{s^2 + 1}$$ Multiply through: $1 = A(s^2+1) + (Bs+C)(s+1)$. Set $s = -1$: $1 = A(2) \Rightarrow A = \frac{1}{2}$. Expand: $1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(s^2+1) + (Bs+C)(s+1) = \tfrac{1}{2}s^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} + Bs^2 + Bs + Cs + C$. Match coefficients: - $s^2$: $\tfrac{1}{2} + B = 0 \Rightarrow B = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ - $s^0$: $\tfrac{1}{2} + C = 1 \Rightarrow C = \tfrac{1}{2}$ **Step 4:** Rewrite. $$Y(s) = \frac{1/2}{s+1} + \frac{-\tfrac{1}{2}s + \tfrac{1}{2}}{s^2 + 1} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{s}{s^2+1} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{s^2+1}$$ **Step 5:** Inverse transform term-by-term. $$y(t) = \frac{1}{2} e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2} \cos t + \frac{1}{2} \sin t, \quad t \geq 0$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{y(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-t} - \cos t + \sin t\right) u(t)}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Laplace Transform Process Flow The following Mermaid block illustrates the conceptual pipeline for solving an electrical network problem using the Laplace Transform. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric, and all labels contain only plain uppercase text without markdown formatting. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[TIME DOMAIN
CIRCUIT WITH
DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
AND INITIAL CONDITIONS] --> B[APPLY LAPLACE
TRANSFORM L ON
EACH ELEMENT] B --> C[S DOMAIN
EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT
PURELY ALGEBRAIC
BRANCHES] C --> D[SOLVE USING
OHMS LAW KVL
AND KCL IN S DOMAIN] D --> E[S DOMAIN
RESPONSE FUNCTION
Y OF S] E --> F[PARTIAL FRACTION
EXPANSION OF
RATIONAL EXPRESSION] F --> G[APPLY INVERSE
LAPLACE TRANSFORM
TERM BY TERM] G --> H[TIME DOMAIN
COMPLETE RESPONSE
Y OF T] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Interpretation:** The time-domain differential equation (left) is translated into an algebraic s-domain circuit (center), solved with simple algebra, and translated back (right). This three-step paradigm is the foundation of **modern control theory** and **signal processing**. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: s-Domain Equivalent Elements This diagram maps each physical circuit element to its Laplace-domain equivalent, including the initial-condition source models. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL TIME DOMAIN ELEMENT"] P1[RESISTOR R] P2[INDUCTOR L
WITH INITIAL CURRENT I0] P3[CAPACITOR C
WITH INITIAL VOLTAGE V0] end subgraph SLAP["LAPLACE S DOMAIN EQUIVALENT"] S1[IMPEDANCE Z = R] S2[IMPEDANCE S L
IN SERIES WITH
VOLTAGE SOURCE L I0] S3[IMPEDANCE 1 OVER S C
IN SERIES WITH
VOLTAGE SOURCE V0 OVER S] end P1 --> S1 P2 --> S2 P3 --> S3 style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520 style S2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520 style S3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** In the s-domain, an inductor becomes a series combination of an impedance $sL$ and a voltage source $L \cdot i_L(0^-)$ that represents the energy initially stored. A capacitor becomes a series impedance $1/(sC)$ with a voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$. These sources are critical — ignoring them is the most common KTU board mistake. ## 4.3 Inverse Laplace Transform Decision Tree This flow guides the student through the correct inverse-Laplace technique based on the form of $F(s)$. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[GIVEN F OF S
A RATIONAL FUNCTION] --> B{DEGREE OF
NUMERATOR
VS DENOMINATOR} B -->|NUM LESS DEN| C[PROPER FRACTION
GO TO PARTIAL
FRACTION EXPANSION] B -->|NUM GREATER EQ DEN| D[PERFORM LONG
DIVISION TO
EXTRACT POLYNOMIAL] D --> C C --> E[IDENTIFY
DENOMINATOR ROOTS
POLES] E --> F{ARE POLES
REAL AND
DISTINCT} F -->|YES| G[USE A OVER
S PLUS A FORM
EXPONENTIAL DECAY] F -->|NO REPEATED| H[USE A OVER
S PLUS A RAISED
TO POWER N FORM] F -->|NO COMPLEX| I[USE DAMPED
SINE COSINE
FORM] G --> J[APPLY INVERSE
TRANSFORM TABLE
TERM BY TERM] H --> J I --> J J --> K[FINAL ANSWER
F OF T WITH
U OF T] style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style K fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State and explain the **Initial Value Theorem** and **Final Value Theorem** of the Laplace Transform. Mention the conditions under which they are applicable. **Model Answer:** **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $$\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$$ **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $$\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$$ **Conditions for FVT applicability:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie in the **open left-half of the s-plane** (strictly $\text{Re}(s) < 0$). If any pole lies on the $j\omega$-axis or in the right-half plane, the limit does not exist and the theorem is invalid. **Physical meaning:** IVT gives the response *immediately after* switching (useful for verifying initial capacitor voltages and inductor currents). FVT gives the *steady-state* value without performing a full inverse transform. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Find the Laplace Transform of a **damped sinusoid** $f(t) = e^{-2t}\sin(5t)\, u(t)$ using the **frequency-shifting property**. **Model Answer:** **Step 1:** Recall the transform pair for $\sin(\omega t)$: $$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(5t)\} = \frac{5}{s^2 + 25}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the **Frequency Shifting Property**: $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}f(t)\} = F(s+a)$. Here, $a = 2$, so we replace $s$ with $(s+2)$: $$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-2t}\sin(5t)\} = \frac{5}{(s+2)^2 + 25} = \frac{5}{s^2 + 4s + 29}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{F(s) = \frac{5}{s^2 + 4s + 29}}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Problems (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Find the Inverse Laplace Transform of: $$F(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 5)}$$ **(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove the **Convolution Theorem** for Laplace Transforms. Hence, evaluate $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s^2+1)}\right\}$. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify the poles.** The denominator factors as $(s+1)$ and $(s^2+2s+5) = (s+1)^2 + 4$, which has complex roots at $s = -1 \pm j2$. **Step 2 — Set up partial fraction expansion:** $$\frac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 5)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{Bs + C}{s^2 + 2s + 5}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply through by the denominator:** $$2s^2 + 6s + 5 = A(s^2 + 2s + 5) + (Bs + C)(s+1)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the cover-up rule at $s = -1$:** $$2(1) + 6(-1) + 5 = A(1 - 2 + 5) \;\Rightarrow\; 2 - 6 + 5 = 4A \;\Rightarrow\; 1 = 4A \;\Rightarrow\; A = \frac{1}{4}$$ **Step 5 — Expand and match coefficients.** Expand the right-hand side: $$A s^2 + 2A s + 5A + B s^2 + B s + C s + C = (A+B)s^2 + (2A+B+C)s + (5A + C)$$ Match with $2s^2 + 6s + 5$: - $s^2$ coefficient: $A + B = 2 \Rightarrow B = 2 - \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{7}{4}$ - $s^0$ coefficient: $5A + C = 5 \Rightarrow C = 5 - \tfrac{5}{4} = \tfrac{15}{4}$ **Step 6 — Rewrite $F(s)$:** $$F(s) = \frac{1/4}{s+1} + \frac{\tfrac{7}{4}s + \tfrac{15}{4}}{s^2 + 2s + 5} = \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{s+1} + \frac{7}{4}\cdot\frac{s}{s^2 + 2s + 5} + \frac{15}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 5}$$ **Step 7 — Complete the square in the quadratic denominator:** $$s^2 + 2s + 5 = (s+1)^2 + 4$$ **Step 8 — Rewrite the numerator to match standard forms.** Split the second term: $$\frac{\tfrac{7}{4}s + \tfrac{15}{4}}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2} = \frac{7}{4}\cdot\frac{s+1}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2} + \frac{15/4 - 7/4}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2} = \frac{7}{4}\cdot\frac{s+1}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{2}{(s+1)^2 + 2^2}$$ **Step 9 — Apply inverse Laplace transforms using the frequency-shifted standard pairs:** - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+1}\right\} = e^{-t}$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{s+1}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right\} = e^{-t}\cos(2t)$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{2}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right\} = e^{-t}\sin(2t)$ **Final Answer to Part (a):** $$\boxed{f(t) = \frac{1}{4}e^{-t} + \frac{7}{4}e^{-t}\cos(2t) + \frac{1}{2}e^{-t}\sin(2t), \quad t \geq 0}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) [7 Marks] **Statement:** If $f_1(t)$ and $f_2(t)$ are piecewise continuous and of exponential order, with $\mathcal{L}\{f_1(t)\} = F_1(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{f_2(t)\} = F_2(s)$, then: $$\mathcal{L}\{f_1(t) * f_2(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f_1(\tau) f_2(t-\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s)$$ **Proof:** By the definition of the Laplace transform of the convolution integral: $$I = \int_0^\infty e^{-st} \left[\int_0^t f_1(\tau) f_2(t-\tau)\, d\tau\right] dt$$ Reverse the order of integration. The region of integration is $0 \leq \tau \leq t < \infty$, which is equivalent to $0 \leq \tau < \infty$ and $\tau \leq t < \infty$: $$I = \int_0^\infty f_1(\tau) \left[\int_\tau^\infty e^{-st} f_2(t-\tau)\, dt\right] d\tau$$ Substitute $u = t - \tau$, so $dt = du$ and the inner limits become $0$ to $\infty$: $$I = \int_0^\infty f_1(\tau) \left[\int_0^\infty e^{-s(u+\tau)} f_2(u)\, du\right] d\tau = \int_0^\infty f_1(\tau) e^{-s\tau} d\tau \cdot \int_0^\infty e^{-su} f_2(u)\, du$$ The two factors are exactly $F_1(s)$ and $F_2(s)$: $$\mathcal{L}\{f_1(t) * f_2(t)\} = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s) \qquad \blacksquare$$ **Application — Evaluate $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s^2+1)}\right\}$:** **Step 1:** Recognize that $\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s^2+1)} = \dfrac{1}{s+1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s^2+1}$. **Step 2:** Identify individual inverse transforms: - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+1}\right\} = e^{-t} u(t) = f_1(t)$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2+1}\right\} = \sin(t)\, u(t) = f_2(t)$ **Step 3:** Apply the convolution integral in the time domain: $$f(t) = \int_0^t e^{-\tau} \sin(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$ **Step 4:** Use the standard result or the table: $$\boxed{f(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-t} - \cos t + \sin t\right) u(t)}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Find the Inverse Laplace Transform of: $$F(s) = \frac{s+1}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$$ **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Laplace Transform, solve the differential equation: $$\frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 5\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 6 y(t) = u(t), \quad y(0^-) = 0, \quad \frac{dy(0^-)}{dt} = 1$$ --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Set up partial fractions: $$\frac{s+1}{s(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3}$$ **Step 2:** Multiply by $s(s+2)(s+3)$: $$s + 1 = A(s+2)(s+3) + Bs(s+3) + Cs(s+2)$$ **Step 3 — Cover-up at $s = 0$:** $$0 + 1 = A(2)(3) \;\Rightarrow\; A = \frac{1}{6}$$ **Step 4 — Cover-up at $s = -2$:** $$-2 + 1 = B(-2)(-2+3) \;\Rightarrow\; -1 = -2B \;\Rightarrow\; B = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Cover-up at $s = -3$:** $$-3 + 1 = C(-3)(-3+2) \;\Rightarrow\; -2 = 3C \;\Rightarrow\; C = -\frac{2}{3}$$ **Step 6 — Verify:** $A + B + C$ (residues sum) = $\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1 + 3 - 4}{6} = 0$ ✓ (correct, since numerator degree is less than denominator). **Step 7 — Inverse transform term-by-term:** $$f(t) = \frac{1}{6} u(t) + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} u(t) - \frac{2}{3} e^{-3t} u(t)$$ **Final Answer to Part (a):** $$\boxed{f(t) = \left(\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} - \frac{2}{3}e^{-3t}\right) u(t)}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Apply Laplace Transform to both sides.** Use $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$ and the differentiation theorems. Recall: - $\mathcal{L}\{y''(t)\} = s^2 Y(s) - s y(0^-) - y'(0^-)$ - $\mathcal{L}\{y'(t)\} = s Y(s) - y(0^-)$ - $\mathcal{L}\{y(t)\} = Y(s)$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given initial conditions** $y(0^-) = 0$, $y'(0^-) = 1$: $$s^2 Y(s) - s(0) - 1 + 5\bigl[s Y(s) - 0\bigr] + 6 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$s^2 Y(s) - 1 + 5s Y(s) + 6 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s}$$ $$(s^2 + 5s + 6) Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} + 1 = \frac{1 + s}{s}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $Y(s)$:** $$Y(s) = \frac{s+1}{s(s^2 + 5s + 6)} = \frac{s+1}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$$ **Step 5 — Partial fraction expansion** (same as in Part (a)): $$Y(s) = \frac{1/6}{s} + \frac{1/2}{s+2} - \frac{2/3}{s+3}$$ **Step 6 — Apply inverse Laplace transform:** $$y(t) = \frac{1}{6} u(t) + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} u(t) - \frac{2}{3} e^{-3t} u(t)$$ **Final Answer to Part (b):** $$\boxed{y(t) = \left(\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} - \frac{2}{3} e^{-3t}\right) u(t)}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Laplace Transform Problems (as per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $u(t)$:** The inverse transform is only valid for $t \geq 0$. Missing the $u(t)$ factor costs at least 1 mark. > 2. **Sign errors in cover-up rule:** When finding $B$ at $s = -2$, students often substitute into the *wrong factor*. Always substitute the root into every $(s - \text{root})$ factor, not just one. > 3. **Final Value Theorem misuse:** Applying $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ when $F(s)$ has poles on the $j\omega$-axis (e.g., undamped sinusoidal steady state) is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Initial conditions in derivatives:** Failing to include $-y(0^-)$ and $-y'(0^-)$ when transforming $y''$ and $y'$ results in loss of all 7 marks in a differential-equation problem. > 5. **Not completing the square:** When the denominator is $s^2 + 2s + 5$, students must complete the square to $(s+1)^2 + 4$ before using the $\sin$ and $\cos$ transform pairs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition to memorize:** $F(s) = \int_0^\infty e^{-st} f(t)\, dt$, with inverse $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}$. - **Five indispensable properties:** Linearity, Time Shifting, Frequency Shifting, Differentiation, Integration. - **Two power theorems:** Initial Value Theorem and Final Value Theorem — both used to *avoid* doing the full inverse transform. - **Convolution Theorem:** Time-domain convolution $\Leftrightarrow$ s-domain multiplication. - **Master the partial fraction expansion:** Real distinct roots, repeated real roots, and complex conjugate pairs each require different inverse transform forms. - **Standard transform table to memorize cold:** $u(t) \to 1/s$, $t \to 1/s^2$, $e^{-at} \to 1/(s+a)$, $\sin\omega t \to \omega/(s^2+\omega^2)$, $\cos\omega t \to s/(s^2+\omega^2)$. - **s-domain element models** (extremely high-yield for circuit analysis in Module 2 onward): - Resistor $R \to$ Impedance $R$. - Inductor $L \to$ Impedance $sL$ in series with voltage source $L \cdot i_L(0^-)$. - Capacitor $C \to$ Impedance $1/(sC)$ in series with voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$. - **Pole locations determine the time response:** Real negative pole $\to$ decaying exponential; complex pair with negative real part $\to$ damped sinusoid; pole at origin $\to$ constant or ramp; right-half pole $\to$ unstable growth. - **Always state the ROC** when an examiner specifically asks — it earns the extra half-mark in KTU 2024 scheme valuations. - **Verify your work** with the IVT and FVT wherever possible — they are excellent self-checking tools and frequently asked as standalone 3-mark questions.

topics.title: Transformation of Basic Signals and Circuits into s-Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transformation of Basic Signals and Circuits into s-Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **s-domain transformation** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) electrical network is the process of converting the time-domain circuit elements, sources, and governing differential equations into equivalent algebraic representations in the **complex frequency domain** using the **Laplace Transform**. Here, the complex variable **s = σ + jω** (where σ is the real part representing damping and ω is the imaginary part representing frequency) replaces the time derivative operator **d/dt**.

Mathematically, the unilateral Laplace Transform of a time-domain function **f(t)** is defined as:

$$
F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt
$$

The lower limit of **0⁻** is critical in KTU analysis as it captures the initial conditions (stored energy) of capacitors and inductors at the instant just before switching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why s-Domain?** It converts messy integro-differential equations (hard to solve) into simple algebraic equations (easy to solve) by turning differentiation into multiplication by **s** and integration into division by **s**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to describe the motion of a roller coaster with respect to **time** (t-domain) versus describing it using a **photograph** (s-domain). 

- **Time Domain (t)**: You must watch every bump, dip, and acceleration frame-by-frame.
- **s-Domain**: The photograph compresses the entire ride into a single image showing the "shape" of the motion in terms of how fast it grows (σ) and how fast it oscillates (ω).

In circuits, **resistors are like static objects** (don't change with time), while **inductors and capacitors store energy** like springs. The Laplace Transform essentially "photographs" the energy stored in these springs at t = 0⁻ and converts the springs into equivalent algebraic impedances that obey **Ohm's Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used:**
> - Complex frequency: **s = σ + jω** (units: radians/second, rad/s)
> - Time constant: **τ = 1/σ** (units: seconds, s)
> - Resonant frequency: **ω₀ = √(1/LC)** (rad/s)

---

### 1.3 The Transformation Workflow

The complete s-domain transformation process follows three structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — Identify Initial Conditions:** Determine the voltage across every capacitor and current through every inductor at **t = 0⁻** (just before the switch operates).
2. **Step 2 — Replace Elements:** Substitute each **R, L, C** with its corresponding s-domain impedance model, and each time-domain source with its Laplace representation.
3. **Step 3 — Solve Algebraically:** Apply standard network theorems (KVL, KCL, Thevenin, Norton, Mesh, Nodal) using ordinary algebra in the s-domain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: Why Transformation Works

The Laplace Transform turns the time-derivative operator **d/dt** into simple multiplication by **s** because of the following identity (derived using integration by parts):

$$
\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)
$$

This means a differential equation of order *n* in time becomes a polynomial equation of degree *n* in *s*. The **initial conditions** `f(0⁻)` appear naturally as independent sources in the s-domain circuit.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### **Table 2.1: Laplace Transform Pairs of Basic Signals**

| Time Domain Signal f(t), t ≥ 0 | Laplace Transform F(s) | Region of Convergence (ROC) |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Impulse δ(t) | 1 | All s |
| Unit Step u(t) | 1/s | Re(s) > 0 |
| Unit Ramp t·u(t) | 1/s² | Re(s) > 0 |
| tⁿ·u(t) | n! / sⁿ⁺¹ | Re(s) > 0 |
| Exponential e⁻ᵃᵗ·u(t) | 1 / (s + a) | Re(s) > -a |
| Sinusoid sin(ωt)·u(t) | ω / (s² + ω²) | Re(s) > 0 |
| Cosinusoid cos(ωt)·u(t) | s / (s² + ω²) | Re(s) > 0 |
| Damped Sine e⁻ᵃᵗ·sin(ωt)·u(t) | ω / ((s + a)² + ω²) | Re(s) > -a |
| Damped Cosine e⁻ᵃᵗ·cos(ωt)·u(t) | (s + a) / ((s + a)² + ω²) | Re(s) > -a |
| Hyperbolic Sine sinh(at)·u(t) | a / (s² - a²) | Re(s) > a |
| Hyperbolic Cosine cosh(at)·u(t) | s / (s² - a²) | Re(s) > a |
| Shifted Step u(t - a) | e⁻ᵃˢ / s | Re(s) > 0 |

#### **Table 2.2: s-Domain Element Transformation Models**

| Element | Time Domain | s-Domain Impedance | Series Initial Condition Source | Parallel Initial Condition Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor R | v(t) = Ri(t) | R | None | None |
| Inductor L | v(t) = L·di/dt | sL | Voltage Source = L·iL(0⁻) | Current Source = iL(0⁻)/s |
| Capacitor C | i(t) = C·dv/dt | 1/(sC) | Voltage Source = vC(0⁻)/s | Current Source = C·vC(0⁻) |
| Voltage Source | v(t) | V(s) | Direct substitution | Direct substitution |
| Current Source | i(t) | I(s) | Direct substitution | Direct substitution |

#### **Table 2.3: Operational Transform Properties (Critical for KTU)**

| Property | Time Domain | s-Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Linearity | a·f₁(t) + b·f₂(t) | a·F₁(s) + b·F₂(s) |
| Time Differentiation | df(t)/dt | sF(s) - f(0⁻) |
| Second Derivative | d²f(t)/dt² | s²F(s) - s·f(0⁻) - f′(0⁻) |
| Time Integration | ∫₀ᵗ f(τ) dτ | F(s) / s |
| Frequency Shift | e⁻ᵃᵗ·f(t) | F(s + a) |
| Time Shift | f(t - a)·u(t-a) | e⁻ᵃˢ·F(s) |
| Scaling | f(at) | (1/a)·F(s/a) |
| Initial Value Theorem | lim t→0 f(t) | lim s→∞ s·F(s) |
| Final Value Theorem | lim t→∞ f(t) | lim s→0 s·F(s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Value Theorem Caveat:** Only valid if all poles of **s·F(s)** lie in the **left half of the s-plane** (Re(s) < 0). Using it on unstable systems gives wrong answers — a common KTU error!

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

The s-domain transformation is the **backbone of modern control systems, power system stability analysis, and analog filter design**. Engineers use it to:
- Predict **transient response** of RLC circuits (during switching).
- Analyze **switched capacitor circuits** in IC design.
- Solve **stiff differential equations** in power electronics.
- Design **PID controllers** by inspection of transfer functions **H(s) = Vout(s)/Vin(s)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of the Unit Step Function u(t)

By definition:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \cdot e^{-st}\,dt
$$

**Step 1:** Evaluate the integral directly.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\,dt
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize this as a standard exponential integral of the form ∫eᵃˣ dx = eᵃˣ/a.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the upper and lower limits (assuming Re(s) > 0 so the exponential decays).

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{e^{-sT}}{-s} - \frac{e^{0}}{-s} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify. As T → ∞ with Re(s) > 0, the first term vanishes.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 0 - \left(\frac{1}{-s}\right) = \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s}$ with ROC: Re(s) > 0. ✅

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of the Unit Impulse δ(t)

Using the sifting property of the Dirac delta:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \delta(t) \cdot e^{-st}\,dt
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the sifting property ∫δ(t)·g(t) dt = g(0).

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = e^{-s(0)}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the exponential.

$$
e^0 = 1
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ for all s. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> The impulse is unique because its transform has **no poles** — it represents an instantaneous event with infinite amplitude and zero duration.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of the Exponential e⁻ᵃᵗ·u(t)

$$
\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st}\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\,dt
$$

**Step 1:** Combine the exponentials.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\,dt
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate.

$$
= \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate limits. For Re(s+a) > 0:

$$
= 0 - \frac{1}{-(s+a)} = \frac{1}{s+a}
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ with ROC: Re(s) > -a. ✅

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: s-Domain Impedance of an Inductor (with Initial Current)

The time-domain v-i relationship of an inductor is:

$$
v(t) = L\frac{di(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply Laplace Transform to both sides using the differentiation property.

$$
V(s) = L\left[ sI(s) - i_L(0^-) \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the right-hand side.

$$
V(s) = sL \cdot I(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret physically. The term **sL** is the inductor's impedance. The term **L·iL(0⁻)** represents an independent voltage source in series with the impedance.

$$
V(s) = sL \cdot I(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)
$$

**Result:** Series equivalent model: a **series voltage source of magnitude L·iL(0⁻)** with polarity opposite to the assumed current direction, in series with impedance **sL**. ✅

**Alternative Derivation (Parallel Model):**

From step 2, divide throughout by sL:

$$
\frac{V(s)}{sL} + \frac{i_L(0^-)}{s} = I(s)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
I(s) = \frac{V(s)}{sL} + \frac{i_L(0^-)}{s}
$$

This shows the inductor can also be represented as a **parallel current source of magnitude iL(0⁻)/s** in parallel with impedance **sL**. ✅

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: s-Domain Impedance of a Capacitor (with Initial Voltage)

The time-domain v-i relationship of a capacitor is:

$$
i(t) = C\frac{dv(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace Transform.

$$
I(s) = C\left[ sV(s) - v_C(0^-) \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Expand.

$$
I(s) = sC \cdot V(s) - C \cdot v_C(0^-)
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for V(s).

$$
sC \cdot V(s) = I(s) + C \cdot v_C(0^-)
$$

$$
V(s) = \frac{1}{sC} \cdot I(s) + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}
$$

**Result:** Series equivalent model: a **series voltage source of magnitude vC(0⁻)/s** in series with impedance **1/(sC)**. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for Initial Conditions:**
> - **Inductor** stores current → represents a "current memory" → parallel current source form.
> - **Capacitor** stores voltage → represents a "voltage memory" → series voltage source form.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Laplace Transform of sin(ωt) Using Euler's Identity

Starting from the exponential form:

$$
\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply Laplace Transform line-by-line.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j}\left[\mathcal{L}\{e^{j\omega t}\} - \mathcal{L}\{e^{-j\omega t}\}\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the exponential transform formula 1/(s ± a).

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j}\left[\frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega}\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Combine the fractions over a common denominator.

$$
= \frac{1}{2j}\left[\frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)}\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator and denominator.

$$
= \frac{1}{2j}\left[\frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel 2j.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ with ROC: Re(s) > 0. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transformation Workflow

The following flowchart maps the systematic procedure for converting any LTI circuit from the time domain into the s-domain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Time-Domain LTI Circuit] --> B[Identify All Energy Storage Elements]
    B --> C{Inductor Present?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Determine iL of 0 minus]
    C -->|No| E{Capacitor Present?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F[Determine vC of 0 minus]
    E -->|No| G[No Initial Conditions]
    F --> H[Replace Each R with R]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Replace Each L with sL plus Series Voltage Source L*iL of 0 minus]
    I --> J[Replace Each C with 1 over sC plus Series Voltage Source vC of 0 minus divided by s]
    J --> K[Replace Sources with Their Laplace Transforms]
    K --> L[Apply KVL or KCL in s-Domain]
    L --> M[Solve Algebraic Equations]
    M --> N[Obtain Transfer Function H of s or Output Y of s]
    N --> O[Inverse Laplace Transform if Time Response Needed]
    O --> P[END: Final Solution]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram: s-Domain Element Equivalents

This diagram illustrates the parallel and series equivalent models for energy storage elements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inductor_Models
        A1[Time Domain Inductor v of t equals L times di of t over dt] -->|Laplace Transform| A2[Series Model: sL in series with Voltage Source L times iL of 0 minus]
        A1 -->|Laplace Transform Alt| A3[Parallel Model: sL in parallel with Current Source iL of 0 minus divided by s]
    end
    subgraph Capacitor_Models
        B1[Time Domain Capacitor i of t equals C times dv of t over dt] -->|Laplace Transform| B2[Series Model: 1 over sC in series with Voltage Source vC of 0 minus divided by s]
        B1 -->|Laplace Transform Alt| B3[Parallel Model: 1 over sC in parallel with Current Source C times vC of 0 minus]
    end
    subgraph Resistor_Model
        C1[Time Domain Resistor v equals i times R] -->|Laplace Transform| C2[s-Domain: V of s equals I of s times R, unchanged]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid s-Plane Pole-Zero Layout

A visual representation of how different signals appear as pole-zero plots in the s-plane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sPlane_Layout
        P1[Step Function: Single Pole at Origin] -->|s equals 0| P2[Real Axis Pole]
        P3[Ramp Function: Double Pole at Origin] -->|s equals 0| P4[Double Pole on Real Axis]
        P5[Decaying Exponential: Pole on Negative Real Axis] -->|s equals negative a| P6[Real Axis Pole Left Half Plane]
        P7[Sinusoid: Poles on Imaginary Axis] -->|s equals plus or minus j omega| P8[Conjugate Pole Pair on j omega Axis]
        P9[Damped Oscillation: Poles in Left Half Plane] -->|s equals negative a plus or minus j omega| P10[Conjugate Pole Pair Offset from Origin]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Pole Location:**
> - **Right Half Plane (Re(s) > 0):** System is unstable (output grows exponentially).
> - **Imaginary Axis (Re(s) = 0):** Marginally stable (sustained oscillation).
> - **Left Half Plane (Re(s) < 0):** System is stable (output decays).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Laplace Transform of a function f(t). Mention any two of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The unilateral Laplace Transform of a time-domain function f(t) defined for t ≥ 0 is given by:

$$
F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt
$$

where **s = σ + jω** is the complex frequency variable.

**Two Properties (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Linearity:** $\mathcal{L}\{af_1(t) + bf_2(t)\} = aF_1(s) + bF_2(s)$
2. **Time Differentiation:** $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write the s-domain equivalent circuit for an inductor and a capacitor having initial conditions iL(0⁻) and vC(0⁻) respectively.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Inductor** (1.5 marks): Replaced by impedance **sL** in series with a voltage source of magnitude **L·iL(0⁻)** (or equivalently, in parallel with a current source of magnitude **iL(0⁻)/s**).

- **Capacitor** (1.5 marks): Replaced by impedance **1/(sC)** in series with a voltage source of magnitude **vC(0⁻)/s** (or equivalently, in parallel with a current source of magnitude **C·vC(0⁻)**).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Find the Laplace Transform of the following signals: (7 marks)
   - (i) f(t) = t·e⁻³ᵗ·u(t)
   - (ii) f(t) = e⁻²ᵗ·cos(5t)·u(t)

**(b)** For the circuit shown, the switch is closed at t = 0 with zero initial conditions. Find i(t) using Laplace Transform. Take V = 10V, R₁ = 5Ω, R₂ = 10Ω, L = 2H. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a)(i) — Laplace Transform of f(t) = t·e⁻³ᵗ·u(t)

**Step 1:** Recall the standard transform: $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$

**Step 2:** Apply the **frequency shifting property**: $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}f(t)\} = F(s+a)$

**Step 3:** Substitute F(s) = 1/s² and a = 3.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot e^{-3t}\} = \frac{1}{(s+3)^2}
$$

**Result:** $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+3)^2}$ ✅ (3.5 marks)

#### Part (a)(ii) — Laplace Transform of f(t) = e⁻²ᵗ·cos(5t)·u(t)

**Step 1:** Recall the standard transform: $\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$

**Step 2:** With ω = 5: $\mathcal{L}\{\cos(5t)\} = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + 25}$

**Step 3:** Apply frequency shifting with a = 2.

$$
\mathcal{L}\{e^{-2t}\cos(5t)\} = \frac{s + 2}{(s+2)^2 + 25}
$$

**Result:** $F(s) = \dfrac{s+2}{(s+2)^2 + 25}$ ✅ (3.5 marks)

#### Part (b) — RL Circuit Analysis Using Laplace Transform

**Given:** V = 10V, R₁ = 5Ω, R₂ = 10Ω, L = 2H, iL(0⁻) = 0.

**Step 1:** Draw the s-domain circuit. V(s) = 10/s, R₁ = 5Ω, R₂ = 10Ω, sL = 2s (no initial source since iL(0⁻) = 0).

**Step 2:** Total impedance seen by the source (R₁ + R₂ in series with sL):

$$
Z(s) = R_1 + R_2 + sL = 5 + 10 + 2s = 2s + 15
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's law in s-domain.

$$
I(s) = \frac{V(s)}{Z(s)} = \frac{10/s}{2s + 15}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$
I(s) = \frac{10}{s(2s + 15)} = \frac{5}{s(s + 7.5)}
$$

**Step 5:** Perform partial fraction expansion.

$$
\frac{5}{s(s + 7.5)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s + 7.5}
$$

Solving: A = 5/7.5 = 2/3, B = -2/3.

**Step 6:** Take the inverse Laplace Transform.

$$
I(s) = \frac{2/3}{s} - \frac{2/3}{s + 7.5}
$$

$$
i(t) = \frac{2}{3}(1 - e^{-7.5t}) \cdot u(t) \text{ A}
$$

**Result:** $i(t) = \dfrac{2}{3}\left(1 - e^{-7.5t}\right)u(t)$ A ✅ (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Derive the Laplace Transform of the unit step function u(t) and unit ramp function t·u(t) from first principles. (7 marks)

**(b)** A series RLC circuit has R = 6Ω, L = 1H, C = 1/8 F. The capacitor is initially charged to vC(0⁻) = 4V, and iL(0⁻) = 0. Find the capacitor voltage vC(t) for t > 0 using Laplace Transform. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Unit Step and Ramp Transforms

**Unit Step Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) e^{-st}\,dt = \left[\frac{e^{-st}}{-s}\right]_0^\infty
$$

For Re(s) > 0:

$$
= \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{e^{-sT}}{-s} - \frac{1}{-s} = 0 + \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Result:** $U(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ ✅

**Unit Ramp Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\,dt
$$

Integrate by parts: Let u = t, dv = e⁻ˢᵗ dt → du = dt, v = -e⁻ˢᵗ/s.

$$
= \left[\frac{-t \cdot e^{-st}}{s}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty \frac{e^{-st}}{s}\,dt
$$

The boundary term vanishes for Re(s) > 0:

$$
= 0 + \frac{1}{s}\int_0^\infty e^{-st}\,dt = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2}
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$ ✅

#### Part (b) — Series RLC with Initial Capacitor Voltage

**Given:** R = 6Ω, L = 1H, C = 1/8 F, vC(0⁻) = 4V, iL(0⁻) = 0, source-free (V = 0).

**Step 1:** Write KVL in s-domain for source-free series loop. Impedance series: R + sL + 1/(sC). Initial conditions → series voltage source vC(0⁻)/s = 4/s.

The loop equation:

$$
\left(R + sL + \frac{1}{sC}\right) I(s) = \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values: R = 6, L = 1, C = 1/8 → 1/(sC) = 8/s.

$$
\left(6 + s + \frac{8}{s}\right) I(s) = \frac{4}{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by s.

$$
(s^2 + 6s + 8) I(s) = 4
$$

$$
I(s) = \frac{4}{s^2 + 6s + 8}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor the denominator: s² + 6s + 8 = (s + 2)(s + 4).

$$
I(s) = \frac{4}{(s+2)(s+4)}
$$

**Step 5:** Partial fractions.

$$
\frac{4}{(s+2)(s+4)} = \frac{A}{s+2} + \frac{B}{s+4}
$$

A = 4/(−2+4) = 4/2 = 2, B = 4/(−4+2) = 4/(−2) = −2.

$$
I(s) = \frac{2}{s+2} - \frac{2}{s+4}
$$

**Step 6:** Inverse Laplace:

$$
i(t) = (2e^{-2t} - 2e^{-4t}) \text{ A}
$$

**Step 7:** Find vC(t) using vC = (1/C) ∫i dt. Equivalently, Vc(s) = I(s)/(sC) + vC(0⁻)/s = 8·I(s)/s + 4/s.

$$
V_C(s) = \frac{8 \cdot 4}{s(s+2)(s+4)} + \frac{4}{s} = \frac{32}{s(s+2)(s+4)} + \frac{4}{s}
$$

**Step 8:** Combined partial fraction for vC(t) — directly from initial value theorem, vC(0) = 4V. Final answer:

$$
v_C(t) = \left(4e^{-2t} - 4e^{-4t}\right) \cdot u(t) \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** $v_C(t) = 4(e^{-2t} - e^{-4t})u(t)$ V ✅ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting initial conditions:** Students often draw s-domain circuits assuming iL(0⁻) = 0 and vC(0⁻) = 0 without verifying. Always **read the problem statement carefully**.
> 2. **Wrong polarity of initial sources:** The voltage source L·iL(0⁻) must have polarity such that it would **drive current in the assumed direction of iL** if acting alone. Wrong polarity = wrong sign = lose 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Misapplication of Final Value Theorem:** Using FVT on a function with poles in the right half plane gives a finite (and wrong) answer. Always check ROC first.
> 4. **Confusing t-domain and s-domain:** Writing 1/sC instead of sC or vice-versa. Remember: **Inductor → sL**, **Capacitor → 1/(sC)**.
> 5. **Algebraic errors in partial fractions:** A single sign error here cascades through the entire problem. Double-check by substituting back.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — s-Domain Transformation**

- [x] **Laplace Transform Definition:** $\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$ — uses lower limit 0⁻ to capture initial energy.

- [x] **Core Transform Pairs to Memorize:**
  - δ(t) ↔ **1**
  - u(t) ↔ **1/s**
  - t·u(t) ↔ **1/s²**
  - e⁻ᵃᵗ·u(t) ↔ **1/(s+a)**
  - sin(ωt) ↔ **ω/(s²+ω²)**
  - cos(ωt) ↔ **s/(s²+ω²)**

- [x] **Element Impedances (memorize in this exact form):**
  - Resistor: **Z(s) = R**
  - Inductor: **Z(s) = sL**
  - Capacitor: **Z(s) = 1/(sC)**

- [x] **Initial Condition Models (the trickiest part):**
  - Inductor with current iL(0⁻): **series voltage source = L·iL(0⁻)** OR **parallel current source = iL(0⁻)/s**
  - Capacitor with voltage vC(0⁻): **series voltage source = vC(0⁻)/s** OR **parallel current source = C·vC(0⁻)**

- [x] **Differentiation Property:** $\mathcal{L}\{df/dt\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$ — the **s** multiplies, the **f(0⁻)** is the initial value source.

- [x] **Integration Property:** $\mathcal{L}\{\int f\,dt\} = F(s)/s$

- [x] **Frequency Shift:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}f(t)\} = F(s+a)$ — shift **a** to the **left** in s-plane.

- [x] **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $f(0^+) = \lim_{s\to\infty} sF(s)$ — valid for proper rational functions.

- [x] **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $f(\infty) = \lim_{s\to 0} sF(s)$ — **only valid if Re(all poles of sF(s)) < 0**.

- [x] **Source-Free Response:** Initial condition sources act alone, producing **natural response** with exponents at the system's natural frequencies (poles).

- [x] **Forced Response:** External sources (after Laplace) produce **particular solution** in steady-state.

- [x] **Complete Response:** i(t) = i_natural(t) + i_forced(t), or equivalently via inverse Laplace of total s-domain expression.

- [x] **KTU Exam Tip:** Always redraw the s-domain circuit **before** writing equations — this earns 1-2 marks and prevents confusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transient Analysis of RL and RLC Networks with Impulse, Step, and Sinusoidal Inputs (With and Without Initial Conditions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transient Analysis of RL and RLC Networks with Impulse, Step, and Sinusoidal Inputs (With and Without Initial Conditions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Transient Analysis of RL and RLC Networks

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transient Analysis** is the study of the temporary behavior of electrical networks during the interval between an initial steady state and a new final steady state, immediately following a switching event, sudden application of a forcing function, or a change in circuit conditions.

The **forcing functions (inputs)** standardized under KTU Module 2 are:

- **Unit Step Input:** $u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$
- **Unit Impulse Input:** $\delta(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t \neq 0 \\ \infty, & t = 0 \end{cases}$ with $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$
- **Sinusoidal Input:** $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ or $V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$

The **initial conditions** are the values of inductor currents and capacitor voltages (and their derivatives) at $t = 0^-$ (just before switching) or $t = 0^+$ (just after switching).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tank System 🚰

Imagine a **water tank** connected to a pipe:
- The **inductor (L)** behaves like a **heavy flywheel** in the pipe — it resists sudden changes in flow (current).
- The **capacitor (C)** behaves like an **expansion tank** — it resists sudden changes in pressure (voltage).
- The **resistor (R)** is the **friction in the pipe** — it dissipates energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** Inductors NEVER allow instantaneous change in current, and capacitors NEVER allow instantaneous change in voltage. This is the **Continuity of State Property** and is the foundation of all transient analysis.

When you suddenly open a valve (apply a step input), the water flow (current) does not jump immediately — it ramps up gradually. This gradual transition is the **transient response**. Once it stabilizes, the system reaches the **steady-state response**.

## 1.3 Classification of Responses

| Response Type | Trigger | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural (Source-Free) Response** | Initial energy in L and C, no source | $x_n(t) = K_1 e^{s_1 t} + K_2 e^{s_2 t}$ |
| **Forced (Particular) Response** | External source active | Depends on source type |
| **Complete Response** | Both initial energy and source | $x(t) = x_n(t) + x_f(t)$ |
| **Step Response** | Sudden DC activation | $s(t) = x(\infty) + [x(0^+) - x(\infty)]e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| **Impulse Response** | Brief impulsive excitation | $h(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}s(t)$ |
| **Sinusoidal Response** | $V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ applied | $x_{ss}(t) + x_{tr}(t)$ |

# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Differential Equation Framework

Any first-order RL or RC network obeys:

$$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} x(t) = f(t)$$

Any second-order RLC network obeys:

$$\frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + \omega_0^2 \, x(t) = f(t)$$

where the **characteristic equation** is $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$, giving roots:

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

## 2.2 The Three Damping Regimes (Second-Order RLC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damping Coefficient $\alpha$** vs **Resonant Frequency $\omega_0$** determines the qualitative nature of the response.

| Regime | Condition | Roots | Response Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overdamped** | $\alpha > \omega_0$ | Real, distinct, negative | Slow exponential rise, no oscillation |
| **Critically Damped** | $\alpha = \omega_0$ | Real, repeated, negative | Fastest non-oscillatory response |
| **Underdamped** | $\alpha < \omega_0$ | Complex conjugates | Damped sinusoidal oscillation |

The **damped natural frequency** is defined as:

$$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

### First-Order Networks

| Quantity | RL Circuit | RC Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Time constant $\tau$ | $L / R$ | $R C$ |
| Natural response | $K e^{-t/\tau}$ | $K e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| Step response (zero IC) | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-Rt/L}\right)$ | $v_C(t) = V_s\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$ |
| Step response (non-zero IC $I_0$ or $V_0$) | $i(t) = I_{\infty} + (I_0 - I_{\infty})e^{-Rt/L}$ | $v_C(t) = V_{\infty} + (V_0 - V_{\infty})e^{-t/RC}$ |
| Impulse response | $h(t) = \dfrac{1}{L} e^{-Rt/L} u(t)$ | $h(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC} e^{-t/RC} u(t)$ |

### Second-Order Networks (Series RLC)

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Neper frequency $\alpha$ | $R / (2L)$ |
| Resonant frequency $\omega_0$ | $1 / \sqrt{LC}$ |
| Characteristic roots $s_{1,2}$ | $-\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$ |
| Damped frequency $\omega_d$ | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ |
| Quality factor $Q$ | $\omega_0 / (2\alpha) = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ |

### Complete Response Forms

| Regime | Capacitor Voltage $v_C(t)$ for Step Input |
|---|---|
| Overdamped | $v_C(t) = V_s + A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ |
| Critically Damped | $v_C(t) = V_s + (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\alpha t}$ |
| Underdamped | $v_C(t) = V_s + e^{-\alpha t}\left[B_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + B_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right]$ |

### Sinusoidal Response Decomposition

For input $v_s(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$:

$$v_C(t) = \underbrace{V_m \cdot \vert H(j\omega) \vert \cos(\omega t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega))}_{\text{Steady-State (Forced)}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Transient terms}}_{\text{Natural response}}$$

where $H(j\omega)$ is the **transfer function** evaluated at $s = j\omega$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Switching Power Supplies:** RLC transients determine inrush currents and voltage overshoots.
> - **Oscilloscope Probes:** Compensation networks are designed to be *critically damped* for accurate measurement.
> - **Automotive Ignition:** LC tank circuits generate high-voltage spark transients.
> - **Communication Filters:** Impulse response characterizes how a filter processes information symbols.
> - **Protective Relays:** Sinusoidal transient analysis is fundamental to overcurrent coordination.

# 🧮 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Step Response of Series RL Circuit with Initial Current

**Problem:** A series RL circuit has a DC source $V_s$ switched ON at $t = 0$. The inductor carries initial current $i_L(0^-) = I_0$. Find $i_L(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

### Circuit Equation (KVL)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop:

$$V_s = v_R(t) + v_L(t) = R \, i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

### Rearranging into Standard Form

Divide by $L$:

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{R}{L} i(t) = \frac{V_s}{L}$$

### Identifying the Time Constant

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R}, \quad \text{so} \quad \frac{R}{L} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

### Solving the ODE (Method: Integrating Factor)

The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{t/\tau}$. Multiply both sides:

$$e^{t/\tau} \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} e^{t/\tau} i = \frac{V_s}{L} e^{t/\tau}$$

The left side is the exact derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[i(t) \, e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{V_s}{L} e^{t/\tau}$$

### Integrating Both Sides from 0 to t

$$\int_0^t \frac{d}{d\sigma}\left[i(\sigma) e^{\sigma/\tau}\right] d\sigma = \frac{V_s}{L} \int_0^t e^{\sigma/\tau} d\sigma$$

$$i(t) e^{t/\tau} - i(0^+) = \frac{V_s}{L} \cdot \tau \left[e^{\sigma/\tau}\right]_0^t = \frac{V_s \tau}{L} \left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

### Isolating i(t)

$$i(t) e^{t/\tau} = I_0 + \frac{V_s \tau}{L}\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

$$i(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

### Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} + \left(I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}\right) e^{-Rt/L}, \quad t \geq 0\,}$$

**Verification:**
- At $t = 0^+$: $i(0^+) = V_s/R + (I_0 - V_s/R)(1) = I_0$ ✓ (continuity of inductor current)
- At $t \to \infty$: $i(\infty) = V_s/R$ ✓ (steady-state DC: inductor → short circuit)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Step Response of Series RC Circuit (Zero Initial Voltage)

**Problem:** Switch closes at $t = 0$, applying $V_s$ across series R-C with $v_C(0^-) = 0$.

### KVL Equation

$$V_s = R i(t) + v_C(t)$$

### Substituting Capacitor i-v Relation

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

$$V_s = RC \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

### Standard Form

$$\frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC} v_C = \frac{V_s}{RC}$$

### Solving by Separation

The homogeneous solution is $v_{C,h} = A e^{-t/RC}$.

The particular (forced) solution for DC input is $v_{C,p} = V_s$.

### Applying Initial Condition

$$v_C(0^+) = 0 = A + V_s \Rightarrow A = -V_s$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,v_C(t) = V_s\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right), \quad i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} e^{-t/RC}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Source-Free Series RLC (Natural Response)

**Problem:** Capacitor initially charged to $V_0$ discharges through R and L. Find $v_C(t)$.

### KVL on the Loop

$$0 = R i + L \frac{di}{dt} + v_C$$

### Substituting $i = C \dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$

$$LC \frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + RC \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = 0$$

### Characteristic Equation

$$LC \, s^2 + RC \, s + 1 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$

### Defining Parameters

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}, \quad \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

### Three Cases

**Case 1 — Overdamped ($\alpha > \omega_0$):** Real distinct roots $s_1, s_2$

$$v_C(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$$

**Case 2 — Critically Damped ($\alpha = \omega_0$):** Repeated root $s = -\alpha$

$$v_C(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\alpha t}$$

**Case 3 — Underdamped ($\alpha < \omega_0$):** Complex roots $s = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$

$$v_C(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[B_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + B_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

### Constants Determined by Initial Conditions

For natural response, typically: $v_C(0^+) = V_0$ and $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = -\dfrac{i(0^+)}{C} = -\dfrac{I_0}{C}$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Step Response of Underdamped Series RLC

**Problem:** A DC source $V_s$ is applied to a series RLC at $t=0$. Find $v_C(t)$ assuming underdamped case.

### Differential Equation

The forced response is $v_{C,f} = V_s$ (DC steady state).

The transient part uses the underdamped natural form.

### General Solution

$$v_C(t) = V_s + e^{-\alpha t}\left[B_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + B_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

### Apply Initial Condition 1: $v_C(0^+) = 0$

$$0 = V_s + B_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad B_1 = -V_s$$

### Apply Initial Condition 2: $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = 0$ (no initial inductor current → $i_C(0^+) = 0 \Rightarrow dv_C/dt = 0$)

$$\frac{dv_C}{dt} = -\alpha e^{-\alpha t}\left[B_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + B_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right] + e^{-\alpha t}\left[-B_1 \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t) + B_2 \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)\right]$$

At $t = 0^+$:

$$0 = -\alpha B_1 + \omega_d B_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad B_2 = \frac{\alpha V_s}{\omega_d}$$

### Final Underdamped Step Response

$$\boxed{\,v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - e^{-\alpha t}\left(\cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{\alpha}{\omega_d} \sin(\omega_d t)\right)\right]\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Impulse Response of RC Circuit

**Problem:** A unit impulse of current $i_s(t) = \delta(t)$ charges a capacitor C through resistor R. Find $v_C(t)$.

### Governing Equation

The impulse deposits charge $Q = \int_0^{0^+} \delta(t) dt = 1$ coulomb onto the capacitor in zero time. So:

$$v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-) + \frac{1}{C}\int_{0^-}^{0^+} i_C \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{C}$$

### For $t > 0$, Source-Free RC

$$RC \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad v_C(t) = A e^{-t/RC}$$

### Apply Initial Condition

$$v_C(0^+) = \frac{1}{C} = A$$

### Impulse Response

$$\boxed{\,h(t) = v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} e^{-t/RC} \, u(t)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The impulse response is the **time derivative of the step response**. This is a fundamental property: $h(t) = \dfrac{ds(t)}{dt}$. Verify: $s(t) = 1 - e^{-t/RC}$, then $\dfrac{ds}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{RC}e^{-t/RC}$ — this is the impulse response for **voltage across resistor** in an RC circuit driven by an impulse voltage source.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Sinusoidal Response of RL Circuit (Zero Initial Conditions)

**Problem:** $v_s(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$ applied to series RL at $t=0$. Find $i(t)$.

### ODE Setup

$$L \frac{di}{dt} + R i = V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

### Steady-State (Phasor) Solution

Impedance: $Z = R + j\omega L = \vert Z \vert \angle \theta$ where $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}$ and $\theta = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$.

Phasor current: $\mathbf{I} = \dfrac{V_m \angle 0^\circ}{\vert Z \vert \angle \theta} = \dfrac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \angle{-\theta}$

$$i_{ss}(t) = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}} \cos(\omega t - \theta)$$

### Natural (Transient) Response

$$i_n(t) = A e^{-Rt/L}$$

### Complete Response with $i(0^+) = 0$

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert}\cos(-\theta) + A = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert}\cos\theta + A$$

$$A = -\frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert}\cos\theta = -\frac{V_m R}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}$$

### Final Complete Sinusoidal Response

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}} \cos(\omega t - \theta) - \frac{V_m R}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2} e^{-Rt/L}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponential term decays to zero as $t \to \infty$, leaving only the sinusoidal steady-state. The transient vanishes within approximately $5\tau$.

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transient Response Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Applied at t = 0] --> B{System Order}
    B -->|Order 1| C[First-Order Network]
    B -->|Order 2| D[Second-Order Network]
    C --> E[Time Constant tau = L/R or RC]
    E --> F[Exponential Response]
    F --> F1[Zero IC: x t = Xinf 1 minus e to power minus t by tau]
    F --> F2[Nonzero IC: x t = Xinf plus X0 minus Xinf e to power minus t by tau]
    D --> G{alpha vs omega sub 0}
    G -->|alpha greater than omega sub 0| H[Overdamped: Real Distinct Roots]
    G -->|alpha equals omega sub 0| I[Critically Damped: Repeated Root]
    G -->|alpha less than omega sub 0| J[Underdamped: Complex Conjugate Roots]
    H --> H1[Sum of two exponentials]
    I --> I1[Polynomial times exponential]
    J --> J1[Damped sinusoid]
    F1 --> K[Complete Response]
    F2 --> K
    H1 --> K
    I1 --> K
    J1 --> K
    K --> L[Total Response x t]
```

## 4.2 Response Component Architecture (Complete Response = Natural + Forced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Source]
        SI[Step u of t]
        II[Impulse delta of t]
        SinI[Sinusoid Vm cos omega t]
    end
    subgraph SYS[Linear Network]
        N1[Network RL or RC]
        N2[Network RLC]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Response]
        NR[Natural Response K1 e to power s1 t plus K2 e to power s2 t]
        FR[Forced Response Depends on source]
    end
    subgraph SUM[Superposition]
        CR[Complete Response NR plus FR]
    end
    SI --> N1
    II --> N1
    SinI --> N1
    SI --> N2
    II --> N2
    SinI --> N2
    N1 --> NR
    N2 --> NR
    N1 --> FR
    N2 --> FR
    NR --> CR
    FR --> CR
```

## 4.3 Second-Order Damping Regime Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given R, L, C values] --> Compute[Compute alpha = R by 2L and omega sub 0 = 1 by root LC]
    Compute --> Compare{alpha squared minus omega sub 0 squared}
    Compare -->|positive value| OD[Overdamped Regime]
    Compare -->|zero value| CD[Critically Damped Regime]
    Compare -->|negative value| UD[Underdamped Regime]
    OD --> ODResp[vC of t = A1 e to power s1 t plus A2 e to power s2 t]
    CD --> CDResp[vC of t = A1 plus A2 t times e to power minus alpha t]
    UD --> UDResp[vC of t = e to power minus alpha t times B1 cos omega sub d t plus B2 sin omega sub d t]
```

## 4.4 Source-to-Response Mapping (Forcing Function vs Output)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Type of Forcing Function] --> B1[Step Input DC]
    A --> B2[Impulse Input]
    A --> B3[Sinusoidal Input]
    B1 --> C1[Steady state value plus transient exponential]
    B2 --> C2[Pure exponential decay h of t]
    B3 --> C3[Phasor steady state plus decaying transient]
    C1 --> D1[Order 1: One time constant]
    C1 --> D2[Order 2: Two exponentials OR damped sinusoid]
    C2 --> E1[Order 1: Single exponential]
    C2 --> E2[Order 2: Sum of two exponentials OR damped sinusoid]
    C3 --> F1[Order 1: Sinusoid with phase shift plus DC offset decay]
    C3 --> F2[Order 2: Sinusoid plus damped oscillation]
```

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the time constant of an RL circuit. For a series RL circuit with $R = 10\,\Omega$ and $L = 2\,H$, find the time taken for the current to reach 90% of its final value when a DC source is suddenly applied. Assume zero initial conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The time constant $\tau$ of an RL circuit is the time required for the transient response to decay to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its initial value. It is defined as $\tau = L / R$.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 \, \text{s}$$

For step response: $i(t) = I_\infty (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. For $i(t) = 0.9 I_\infty$:

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(10) \approx 2.303$$

$$\boxed{t = 2.303 \times 0.2 = 0.4606 \, \text{s} \approx 460.6 \, \text{ms}}$$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between natural response and forced response. What is the form of the natural response of a critically damped second-order circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Natural Response | Forced Response |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Initial energy in L and C | External forcing function |
| **Form** | Exponentials (or damped sinusoids) | Mirrors the source shape |
| **Time behavior** | Decays with time | Persists as long as source is ON |

**Critically Damped Natural Response Form (2 marks):**

When $\alpha = \omega_0$, the characteristic equation has a **repeated root** $s = -\alpha$. The general form is:

$$x_n(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) \, e^{-\alpha t}$$

This form represents the **fastest non-oscillatory decay** — it is the boundary between oscillation and pure exponential decay.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A series RLC circuit has $R = 6\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,H$, and $C = 1/8\,F$. A DC voltage of $V_s = 24\,V$ is applied at $t = 0$. The initial conditions are $i_L(0^-) = 0$ and $v_C(0^-) = 0$.**

#### **(a)** Derive the differential equation for the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$ and identify the damping regime. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$V_s = v_R + v_L + v_C = R i + L \frac{di}{dt} + v_C$$

**Step 2 — Use capacitor i-v relation** $i = C \dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$:

$$V_s = RC \frac{dv_C}{dt} + LC \frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + v_C$$

**Step 3 — Standard second-order form:**

$$\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L} \frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC} v_C = \frac{V_s}{LC}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + 6 \frac{dv_C}{dt} + 8 v_C = 24 \times 8 = 192$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 \times 1/8}} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compare:** $\alpha = 3 > \omega_0 = 2.828$ → **Overdamped regime** ✓

**Step 7 — Find roots of characteristic equation** $s^2 + 6s + 8 = 0$:

$$(s+2)(s+4) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s_1 = -2, \quad s_2 = -4$$

---

#### **(b)** Determine $v_C(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ and compute the time at which $v_C$ first equals 20 V. (7 marks)
