> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Passive Filters - Filter Fundamentals # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Electrical Filter? An **electrical filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to pass signals within a specified range of frequencies (the **passband**) while attenuating (rejecting) signals outside this range (the **stopband**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"A filter is a reactive network that passes a specified band of frequencies while attenuating all frequencies outside this band."* In the **frequency domain**, a filter's behavior is fully described by its **transfer function** $H(j\omega)$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)} = \vert H(j\omega) \vert \, \angle \, \phi(\omega) $$ where: - $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ → **Magnitude response** (gain / attenuation) - $\phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega)$ → **Phase response** (delay / distortion) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strainer in a Pipe" Imagine pouring a mixture of **coarse sand and fine clay** into a kitchen strainer held over a bucket. The strainer physically *passes* the fine clay (small particles) into the bucket below, while *rejecting* (blocking) the coarse sand (large particles). A **filter** does exactly the same thing — but with **electrical signals instead of particles, and frequency instead of physical size**: | Kitchen Strainer Analogy | Electrical Filter Equivalent | |---|---| | Mixture poured in | Input signal (sum of many frequencies) | | Fine clay passes through | Frequencies inside the **passband** | | Coarse sand held back | Frequencies inside the **stopband** | | Mesh size (small/large holes) | **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ | | Type of strainer (sieve, colander…) | Filter **type** (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** > Passive filters use only **resistors (R), inductors (L), and capacitors (C)**. They require **no external power supply**, dissipate signal energy, and cannot provide gain (gain $\le 1$ always). ## 1.3 Why Do We Need Filters? Filters are fundamental building blocks in: - **Communication systems** → selecting a desired radio channel while rejecting adjacent ones. - **Audio engineering** → separating bass (woofer) from treble (tweeter) signals via crossover networks. - **Power systems** → suppressing harmonics produced by nonlinear loads (rectifiers, SMPS). - **Instrumentation** → removing **50 Hz power-line hum** from biomedical signals (ECG, EEG). - **Data converters** → anti-aliasing (before ADC) and reconstruction (after DAC). ## 1.4 Classification of Filters ### A. By Physical Construction | Class | Components | Requires Power? | Provides Gain? | |---|---|---|---| | **Passive Filter** | R, L, C only | **No** | **No** (Loss only) | | **Active Filter** | R, L, C + Op-Amp / Transistor | **Yes (Vcc / Vee)** | **Yes** | ### B. By Frequency-Selectivity Function | Filter Type | Symbol | Passes | Rejects | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | 🔻 | $0 \to f_c$ | $f > f_c$ | Audio bass, anti-aliasing | | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | 🔺 | $f > f_c$ | $0 \to f_c$ | DC blocker, treble boost | | **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | ⛰️ | $f_L \to f_H$ | Outside band | Radio tuner, channel select | | **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** / Notch | 🏞️ | Outside band | $f_L \to f_H$ | 50 Hz hum rejection | ### C. By Approximation (Response Shape) - **Butterworth** → Maximally flat magnitude in passband. - **Chebyshev** → Sharper cutoff, but has passband ripple. - **Bessel** → Maximally flat group delay (linear phase). - **Elliptic (Cauer)** → Sharpest cutoff with ripple in both bands. ## 1.5 The Concept of Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$)** is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its maximum passband value. In decibels, this corresponds to a drop of: $$ 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -3.0103 \,\text{dB} \approx \mathbf{-3\,dB} $$ Hence $f_c$ is universally called the **"-3 dB frequency"**, **"half-power frequency"**, or **"corner frequency"**. At $f_c$, the output **power** is exactly half the maximum (since $P \propto V^2$, and $V$ falls by $1/\sqrt{2}$). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Filter Response A practical filter's magnitude response $|\,H(j\omega)\,|$ is divided into three distinct regions. Consider an LPF as a working example: ``` |H(jω)| 1.0 ┤━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ ← PASSBAND (signal passes, |H| ≈ 1) │ ┃ 0.707┤- - - - - - -╂━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ -3 dB line │ ┃ ╲ ← TRANSITION BAND (signal attenuated) 0 ┤ ┃ ╲___________ └─────────────┸────────────────────── ω ωc ``` | Region | Frequency Range | Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Passband** | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx 1$ (0 dB) | | **Transition Band** | $\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_s$ | Gradual roll-off from 0 dB → stopband level | | **Stopband** | $\omega \ge \omega_s$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \le A_{min}$ (highly attenuated) | ## 2.2 Filter Order and Roll-Off Rate > [!IMPORTANT] > **Filter Order ($N$)** = the highest power of $s$ (or $\omega$) in the denominator of the transfer function $H(s)$. The **roll-off rate** (asymptotic attenuation slope beyond $\omega_c$) is: $$ \text{Roll-off} = 20 \cdot N \quad \text{dB/decade} \quad = \quad 6 \cdot N \quad \text{dB/octave} $$ | Order $N$ | Roll-off (dB/decade) | Roll-off (dB/octave) | Poles | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 20 | 6 | 1 | | 2 | 40 | 12 | 2 | | 3 | 60 | 18 | 3 | | 4 | 80 | 24 | 4 | | $N$ | $20N$ | $6N$ | $N$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** A higher-order filter gives a *sharper* transition from passband to stopband, but at the cost of: > - More components (cost ↑) > - More phase distortion (group delay non-linearity ↑) > - Greater sensitivity to component tolerances (Q-factor of poles ↑) ## 2.3 Butterworth Filter Approximation — The "Gold Standard" The **Butterworth filter** is the most commonly designed filter in KTU examinations because of its **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband. ### Magnitude-Squared Function For a low-pass Butterworth filter of order $N$ with cutoff $\omega_c$: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$ ### Key Properties 1. **At DC** ($\omega = 0$): $\vert H \vert^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \vert H \vert = 1$ (unity gain). 2. **At cutoff** ($\omega = \omega_c$): $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ($-3$ dB **for all $N$**). 3. **At infinity** ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert H \vert^2 \to 0$ (full attenuation). 4. **Flatness**: The first $(2N-1)$ derivatives of $\vert H \vert^2$ are zero at $\omega = 0$, ensuring maximum passband flatness. ### First Few Orders Explicitly | Order $N$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2$ | Roll-off (dB/dec) | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^2}$ | 20 | | 2 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^4}$ | 40 | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^6}$ | 60 | | $N$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$ | $20N$ | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bookmark this table — every KTU exam question on filters is built from these formulas.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Transfer Function | $H(j\omega) = V_o/V_i$ | dimensionless | complex ratio | | 2 | Magnitude (dB) | $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$ | dB | negative ⇒ attenuation | | 3 | Phase | $\phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega)$ | radians or degrees | | | 4 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF) RC | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | $-3$ dB point | | 5 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF) RL | $f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$ | Hz | $-3$ dB point | | 6 | Roll-off rate | $20N$ dB/decade | dB/dec | $N$ = order | | 7 | Butterworth $\vert H \vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$ | — | unitless | | 8 | Quality factor $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_c}{BW}$ | — | BPF only | | 9 | Bandwidth $BW$ | $f_H - f_L$ | Hz | BPF | | 10 | Pole magnitude Butterworth | $\omega_k = \omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$ | rad/s | $k = 1,2,\dots,N$ | | 11 | Group delay | $\tau_g = -\dfrac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega}$ | seconds | constant ⇒ linear phase | | 12 | Phase delay | $\tau_p = -\dfrac{\phi(\omega)}{\omega}$ | seconds | | > **Note on Table Notation:** In every row above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (magnitude). The vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to prevent conflicts with markdown table pipes. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Filter Fundamentals | Engineering Field | Application | Filter Type Used | |---|---|---| | Power Quality | Suppression of harmonic currents from VFDs | Passive L-C tuned LPF | | Audio Crossovers | Splitting audio into woofer/tweeter feeds | 2nd-order LPF + HPF | | RF Communications | Channel selection in superheterodyne receivers | Multi-pole BPF | | Biomedical | Removing 50 Hz mains interference from ECG | Notch (BSF) | | DAC Reconstruction | Smoothing staircase output of DAC | LPF (anti-image) | | ADC Anti-Aliasing | Band-limiting input before sampling | LPF | ## 2.6 Why "Passive" Filters Are First in the KTU Syllabus Passive filters are studied **first** because: 1. They are built from R, L, C — the same components already mastered in earlier modules. 2. They form the **theoretical foundation** for active filter design (op-amp circuits later replace bulky inductors with simulated inductance / FDNR techniques). 3. They are still the **preferred solution** at high power levels (kW range) where active filters cannot operate. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: Cutoff Frequency of First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter **Circuit:** Series R and C, output taken across the capacitor. ``` R C Vin ──/\/\/──┬──┤├──┬──── Vout │ │ ─┴─ ─┴─ GND GND ``` ### Step 1: Apply Voltage Divider Rule The impedance of the resistor is $Z_R = R$ and the capacitor is $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$. $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute and Simplify $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ ### Step 3: Express Magnitude $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the $-3$ dB Definition Set $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$ Square both sides: $$ 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 $$ $$ (\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 $$ $$ \omega_c RC = 1 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}\;} $$ ### Step 5: Verify Limiting Cases (Sanity Check) - **At $\omega = 0$ (DC):** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+0}} = 1$ ✓ (capacitor is open-circuit, full DC passes). - **At $\omega \to \infty$:** $\vert H \vert \to \dfrac{1}{\infty} = 0$ ✓ (capacitor is short-circuit, output is zero). - **At $\omega = \omega_c$:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = -3$ dB ✓. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Roll-Off Rate of $N$-th Order Butterworth LPF ### Step 1: Write the Magnitude-Squared Function $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$ ### Step 2: Take the Logarithm (to convert to dB) $$ A_{dB}(\omega) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}\right] $$ ### Step 3: Approximation at High Frequencies ($\omega \gg \omega_c$) For $\omega \gg \omega_c$, the term $(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N} \gg 1$, so: $$ A_{dB}(\omega) \approx -10 \log_{10}\!\left[(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}\right] = -10 \cdot 2N \cdot \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c) $$ $$ \boxed{\;A_{dB}(\omega) \approx -20N \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c) \quad \text{for } \omega \gg \omega_c\;} $$ ### Step 4: Interpret as Slope When $\omega$ increases by a factor of 10 (one **decade**), $\log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c)$ increases by 1, so $A_{dB}$ changes by $-20N$ dB. **Conclusion:** Roll-off = $-20N$ dB/decade = $-6N$ dB/octave. --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: Phase Response of First-Order RC LPF From Step 2 of Derivation #1: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} = \frac{1}{1 + j(\omega/\omega_c)} $$ Phase: $$ \phi(\omega) = \angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right) $$ | $\omega$ | $\phi(\omega)$ | |---|---| | 0 | $0°$ | | $\omega_c$ | $-45°$ | | $10\,\omega_c$ | $-84.3°$ | | $\infty$ | $-90°$ | The phase decreases monotonically from $0°$ to $-90°$ — characteristic of a single-pole system. --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4: Butterworth Pole Locations Poles of $H(s)H(-s)$ are found from: $$ 1 + \left(\frac{s}{j\omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad s^{2N} = (-1)^{N+1}\,\omega_c^{2N} $$ The $2N$ roots lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the $s$-plane, **symmetric about both axes**, and *none on the imaginary axis* (for stability of a passive network, the $N$ left-half-plane roots are selected). $$ \boxed{\;s_k = \omega_c \cdot \exp\!\left[j\,\frac{\pi(2k + N - 1)}{2N}\right], \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N\;} $$ For stability, we retain only poles with $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$, i.e., the left-half-plane roots. ### Example: $N = 3$ (3rd-Order Butterworth) $$ s_k = \omega_c \cdot \exp\!\left[j\,\frac{\pi(2k + 2)}{6}\right], \quad k = 1, \ldots, 6 $$ | $k$ | Angle (°) | $s_k$ (in units of $\omega_c$) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 60° | $\omega_c(-0.5 + j0.866)$ | | 2 | 120° | $\omega_c(-1 + j0)$ | | 3 | 180° | $\omega_c(-0.5 - j0.866)$ | | 4 | 240° | $\omega_c(0.5 - j0.866)$ | | 5 | 300° | $\omega_c(1 - j0)$ | | 6 | 360° | $\omega_c(0.5 + j0.866)$ | **LHP poles** (stable): $k = 1, 2, 3$ → choose these. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Filter Types | Property | Butterworth | Chebyshev | Bessel | Elliptic | |---|---|---|---|---| | Passband | Maximally flat | Equi-ripple | Flat | Equi-ripple | | Stopband | Monotonic | Monotonic | Monotonic | Equi-ripple | | Phase / Group delay | Moderate non-linearity | Most non-linear | **Maximally linear** | Highly non-linear | | Roll-off sharpness (same $N$) | Moderate | **Sharpest** | Gentle | **Sharpest of all** | | Pole locations | Circle | Ellipse | — | — | | KTU exam prevalence | **Highest** | Medium | Low | Lowest | | Best use | General purpose | Sharp cutoff needed | Pulse / data signals | Ultra-sharp cutoff | --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style **Problem:** A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter has cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz. Find the attenuation (in dB) at $f = 10$ kHz. ### Solution **Step 1:** Identify parameters: $N = 2$, $f = 10 f_c$. **Step 2:** Apply Butterworth magnitude formula: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^4} = \frac{1}{1 + (10)^4} = \frac{1}{10001} $$ **Step 3:** Compute magnitude: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10001}} \approx \frac{1}{100.005} \approx 0.0099995 $$ **Step 4:** Convert to dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0099995) = -20 \log_{10}(100.005) \approx -40.0009 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 5:** Cross-check using the roll-off rule: Since $f = 10 f_c$ is **one decade** above $f_c$, the attenuation should be approximately: $$ \text{Roll-off} = -20 \cdot N = -20 \cdot 2 = -40 \text{ dB/decade} $$ ✅ **Excellent agreement!** The exact answer $-40.0009$ dB matches the asymptotic $-40$ dB closely (small error due to the $-3$ dB offset at $f_c$). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with `node`. - No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as node names. - Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting. --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Filter System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE
V IN T OFT]:::inBox nodeB[FILTER NETWORK
TWO PORT PASSIVE L C R]:::filtBox nodeC[LOAD
R LOAD 50 OHM TYPICAL]:::loadBox nodeD[OUTPUT SIGNAL
V OUT FILTERED]:::outBox nodeA -- V IN --> nodeB nodeB -- V OUT --> nodeC nodeC -- RETURN PATH --> nodeA nodeB -.-> nodeE[TRANSFER FUNCTION H J OMEGA
DETERMINED BY COMPONENTS]:::tfBox classDef inBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef filtBox fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef loadBox fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef tfBox fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Filter Selection by Frequency Range ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS[START
IDENTIFY FREQUENCY BAND OF INTEREST]:::startBox nodeQ1{WHAT FREQUENCIES
SHOULD PASS THROUGH}:::decBox nodeLP[LOW PASS FILTER LPF
PASS 0 TO F C]:::lpfBox nodeHP[HIGH PASS FILTER HPF
PASS F C TO INFINITY]:::hpfBox nodeBP[BAND PASS FILTER BPF
PASS F L TO F H]:::bpfBox nodeBS[BAND STOP FILTER BSF
REJECT F L TO F H]:::bsfBox nodeQ2{WHAT ROLL OFF
IS REQUIRED}:::decBox nodeN1[FIRST ORDER N EQUALS 1
20 DB PER DECADE]:::n1Box nodeN2[SECOND ORDER N EQUALS 2
40 DB PER DECADE]:::n2Box nodeN3[HIGHER ORDER N GREATER THAN 2
BUTTERWORTH OR CHEBYSHEV]:::n3Box nodeEND[FILTER SPECIFIED
PROCEED TO COMPONENT DESIGN]:::endBox nodeS --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 -->|DC TO F C| nodeLP nodeQ1 -->|F C TO HIGH| nodeHP nodeQ1 -->|NARROW BAND F L TO F H| nodeBP nodeQ1 -->|REJECT NARROW BAND| nodeBS nodeLP --> nodeQ2 nodeHP --> nodeQ2 nodeBP --> nodeQ2 nodeBS --> nodeQ2 nodeQ2 -->|GENTLE SLOPE OK| nodeN1 nodeQ2 -->|MODERATE SLOPE| nodeN2 nodeQ2 -->|STEEP SLOPE NEEDED| nodeN3 nodeN1 --> nodeEND nodeN2 --> nodeEND nodeN3 --> nodeEND classDef startBox fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decBox fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef lpfBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef hpfBox fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef bpfBox fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef bsfBox fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef n1Box fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef n2Box fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef n3Box fill:#BDBDBD,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef endBox fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram of Butterworth Filter Realization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[STEP 1
SPECIFY F C AND N]:::stepBox nodeB[STEP 2
COMPUTE POLE LOCATIONS
USING BUTTERWORTH FORMULA]:::stepBox nodeC[STEP 3
FORM H S PRODUCT OF
FIRST ORDER SECTIONS]:::stepBox nodeD[STEP 4
NORMALIZE TO OMEGA C EQUALS 1
R EQUALS 1 OHM]:::stepBox nodeE[STEP 5
FREQUENCY AND IMPEDANCE
DENORMALIZATION]:::stepBox nodeF[STEP 6
REALIZE USING L AND C
FROM DENORMALIZED VALUES]:::stepBox nodeG[FINAL CIRCUIT
PASSIVE FILTER NETWORK]:::finalBox nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG classDef stepBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef finalBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.5 Frequency-Response Schematic for the Four Filter Types ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nodeSG1[LOW PASS FILTER] nodeLPF[GRAFH H J OMEGA
UNITY FOR OMEGA LESS THAN OMEGA C
DECAYS FOR OMEGA GREATER THAN OMEGA C]:::lpfStyle end subgraph nodeSG2[HIGH PASS FILTER] nodeHPF[GRAFH H J OMEGA
ZERO FOR OMEGA LESS THAN OMEGA C
UNITY FOR OMEGA GREATER THAN OMEGA C]:::hpfStyle end subgraph nodeSG3[BAND PASS FILTER] nodeBPF[GRAFH H J OMEGA
UNITY FROM OMEGA L TO OMEGA H
ZERO ELSEWHERE]:::bpfStyle end subgraph nodeSG4[BAND STOP FILTER] nodeBSF[GRAFH H J OMEGA
ZERO FROM OMEGA L TO OMEGA H
UNITY ELSEWHERE]:::bsfStyle end classDef lpfStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef hpfStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef bpfStyle fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef bsfStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.6 Pole-Zero Plot (Butterworth, $N = 4$) — Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeJ[J OMEGA IMAGINARY AXIS]:::axisBox nodeR[SIGMA REAL AXIS]:::axisBox nodeP1[POLE 1
OMEGA C EXP J 22.5 DEG]:::poleBox nodeP2[POLE 2
OMEGA C EXP J 67.5 DEG]:::poleBox nodeP3[POLE 3
OMEGA C EXP J 112.5 DEG]:::poleBox nodeP4[POLE 4
OMEGA C EXP J 157.5 DEG]:::poleBox nodeP5[POLE 5 MIRROR
OMEGA C EXP J 202.5 DEG]:::poleMirrorBox nodeP6[POLE 6 MIRROR
OMEGA C EXP J 247.5 DEG]:::poleMirrorBox nodeP7[POLE 7 MIRROR
OMEGA C EXP J 292.5 DEG]:::poleMirrorBox nodeP8[POLE 8 MIRROR
OMEGA C EXP J 337.5 DEG]:::poleMirrorBox nodeCIRC[POLE CIRCLE OF RADIUS OMEGA C
SYMMETRIC ABOUT REAL AXIS]:::circBox nodeP1 --- nodeP2 --- nodeP3 --- nodeP4 nodeP5 --- nodeP6 --- nodeP7 --- nodeP8 classDef axisBox fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef poleBox fill:#1976D2,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFFFFF classDef poleMirrorBox fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef circBox fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Cutoff Frequency. Why is it called the "-3 dB Frequency"? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **cutoff frequency** ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$) of a filter is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function falls to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately **70.7 %**) of its maximum passband value. It is called the **"-3 dB frequency"** because converting this voltage ratio to decibels yields: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}(2) \approx -3.0103 \text{ dB} \approx -3 \text{ dB} $$ It is also called the **half-power frequency** because at $f_c$, the output power is exactly one-half the maximum passband power. --- ### Question 2: State the four main types of filters based on frequency selectivity. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | # | Filter Type | Passes | Rejects | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Frequencies from 0 to $f_c$ | Frequencies above $f_c$ | | 2 | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | Frequencies above $f_c$ | Frequencies from 0 to $f_c$ | | 3 | **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | Frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$ | Frequencies outside this band | | 4 | **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** / Notch | Frequencies outside $(f_L, f_H)$ | Frequencies between $f_L$ and $f_H$ | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order RC low-pass filter. Find its cutoff frequency in terms of $R$ and $C$. Show that the magnitude rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 3rd-order Butterworth low-pass filter has a cutoff frequency of $f_c = 5$ kHz. Calculate: **(i)** the magnitude of the transfer function (in dB) at $f = 50$ kHz, **(ii)** the asymptotic roll-off rate. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** **(a)** Explain the four classical filter approximations (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, Elliptic) with reference to their magnitude response, phase linearity, and roll-off sharpness. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The magnitude-squared function of a low-pass filter is given by: $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/2000\pi)^8} $$ Identify the filter type, find its order, cutoff frequency, and the attenuation in dB at $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s. **(7 Marks)** --- ## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION A ### Solution to A(a) **Step 1 — Circuit Analysis:** Consider the series RC network with output across the capacitor. The impedances are $Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$. **Step 2 — Voltage Divider:** $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C} = \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} $$ **Step 3 — Cutoff Frequency:** Set $\omega_c RC = 1$: $$ \boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}, \qquad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}} $$ **Step 4 — Magnitude:** $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} $$ **Step 5 — Roll-Off at High Frequency:** For $\omega \gg \omega_c$: $$ \bigl| H(j\omega) \bigr| \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC} $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(\omega RC) $$ When $\omega$ increases by factor 10 (one decade), $A_{dB}$ drops by $20$ dB. ∎ --- ### Solution to A(b) **Given:** $N = 3$, $f_c = 5$ kHz, $f = 50$ kHz → ratio $= 10$. **(i) Magnitude at 50 kHz:** $$ \bigl| H(j2\pi \cdot 50000) \bigr|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (10)^{2 \cdot 3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^6} \approx 1 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \bigl| H \bigr| \approx \sqrt{10^{-6}} = 10^{-3} $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = -60 \text{ dB} $$ **(ii) Roll-off Rate:** $$ \text{Roll-off} = 20 N = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ dB/decade} $$ **Cross-check:** A 10× (one-decade) increase above $f_c$ yields exactly $60$ dB drop for a 3rd-order Butterworth — consistent with the $-60$ dB found above. ✅ --- ## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS — QUESTION B ### Solution to B(a) | Property | Butterworth | Chebyshev | Bessel | Elliptic | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Passband** | Maximally flat (no ripple) | Equi-ripple | Flat | Equi-ripple | | **Stopband** | Monotonic | Monotonic | Monotonic | Equi-ripple | | **Phase linearity** | Moderate | Poor | **Best** | Poor | | **Roll-off sharpness (same $N$)** | Moderate | Very sharp | Gentle | **Sharpest** | | **Pole locations** | Circle of radius $\omega_c$ | Ellipse | — | — | | **Best for** | General purpose | Sharp cutoff | Pulse/data | Ultra-sharp cutoff | **Exam Tip:** Mention the defining mathematical feature of each — e.g., Butterworth's maximally flat magnitude comes from the first $(2N-1)$ derivatives being zero at $\omega = 0$. --- ### Solution to B(b) **Given:** $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/2000\pi)^8} $$ **Step 1 — Compare with standard Butterworth form:** $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}} $$ Matching exponents: $\boxed{2N = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 4}$. **Step 2 — Identify filter type:** Magnitude squared form matches **Butterworth** with $N = 4$. **Step 3 — Cutoff frequency:** $$ \omega_c = 2000\pi \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{2000\pi}{2\pi} = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 4 — Attenuation at $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s:** Ratio: $\omega/\omega_c = \dfrac{4000\pi}{2000\pi} = 2$. $$ \vert H \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (2)^8} = \frac{1}{1 + 256} = \frac{1}{257} $$ $$ \vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{257}} \approx \frac{1}{16.031} \approx 0.0624 $$ In dB: $$ A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.0624) \approx -24.1 \text{ dB} $$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing cutoff frequency with $-3$ dB point definitions.** The $-3$ dB definition applies to *Butterworth and Bessel* filters. For Chebyshev filters, the "cutoff" is sometimes taken at the passband-edge ripple (e.g., $-0.5$ dB or $-1$ dB), not $-3$ dB. **Read the question carefully.** > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2N$.** The exponent in the Butterworth magnitude-squared function is $2N$, NOT $N$. Students writing $\dfrac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^N}$ lose full marks. > > 3. **Computing magnitude and phase separately but losing magnitude in dB conversion.** Always compute $\vert H \vert$ first, THEN convert: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H \vert$. > > 4. **Confusing rad/s with Hz in cutoff frequency.** If the question gives $f_c$ in Hz, convert to $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ rad/s **before** plugging into formulas. > > 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units (Hz, rad/s, dB). > > 6. **Active vs Passive confusion.** "Filter Fundamentals" in Module 4 means **passive only** unless explicitly stated. Don't introduce op-amps. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:** - ☐ A **filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network. It does NOT add energy — only redistributes or attenuates it. - ☐ **Passive filters** use only $R$, $L$, $C$. No external power, no gain, always $\vert H \vert \le 1$. - ☐ **Four filter types**: LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF (Notch). - ☐ **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is the $-3$ dB point: $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$, $A_{dB} = -3$ dB. - ☐ For a 1st-order RC LPF: $\;f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. For RL LPF: $\;f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$. - ☐ **Filter order $N$** = highest power of $s$ in denominator. - ☐ **Roll-off rate** $= 20N$ dB/decade $= 6N$ dB/octave. - ☐ **Butterworth** magnitude-squared: $\vert H \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}}$. - ☐ Butterworth has **maximally flat** passband (first $2N-1$ derivatives zero at $\omega = 0$). - ☐ Butterworth poles lie on a **circle of radius $\omega_c$** in the $s$-plane, symmetric about the real axis; only LHP poles are chosen. - ☐ **Chebyshev** = sharper roll-off but passband ripple; **Bessel** = best phase linearity (pulse preservation); **Elliptic** = sharpest cutoff but ripple in both bands. - ☐ **Magnitude in dB:** $\;A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$. **Phase:** $\;\phi = \angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega/\omega_c)$ for 1st-order LPF. - ☐ At $f = f_c$: phase = $-45°$ for any 1st-order LPF. - ☐ Asymptotic magnitude at high $\omega$: $\;A_{dB} \approx -20N \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_c)$. - ☐ **KTU favourite questions**: (i) Derive cutoff frequency of RC LPF/HPF. (ii) Compute attenuation in dB at given frequency for Butterworth. (iii) Compare filter approximations. (iv) Identify filter type, order, and cutoff from given $\vert H \vert^2$.

topics.title: Design of Constant K Low Pass Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT631 - ELECTRICAL NETWORKS & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Constant K Low Pass Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **Constant-K Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a fundamental, classical filter network constructed using the **Image Parameter Method**, in which the product of the series arm impedance ($Z_1$) and the shunt arm impedance ($Z_2$) is a **real, positive, frequency-independent constant**, denoted as $K^2$. This constant is defined as:

$$K^2 = Z_1 \cdot Z_2 = \frac{L}{C} \quad \text{(constant, real, positive)}$$

For a **Low Pass Filter** topology, the configuration is a **reactance network** where:

- The **series arm** contains a pure **inductor** $Z_1 = j\omega L$
- The **shunt arm** contains a pure **capacitor** $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

The name *Constant-K* literally arises because $Z_1 Z_2 = K^2$ holds true for **all frequencies** $\omega$, making $K$ a true design constant chosen at the start of the design procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Constant-K filter is the *foundational prototype* from which all advanced filters (m-derived, composite) are derived. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for both KTU ESE (End Semester) and internal module tests.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at the Club" Model

Imagine an **exclusive nightclub** with two bouncers working together at the entrance:

1. **The Slow-Motion Bouncer (Inductor $L$):** Only allows people who move *slowly* (i.e., low-frequency, slowly changing signals). When someone tries to rush in too fast (high-frequency), the inductor creates a strong *back-EMF* that physically resists them. Mathematically: $X_L = \omega L$, so as $\omega \uparrow$, opposition $\uparrow$.

2. **The Side-Street Drain (Capacitor $C$):** Any fast-mover (high-frequency signal) that sneaks past the inductor gets immediately diverted into a side alley (shunted to ground) by the capacitor. Mathematically: $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$, so as $\omega \uparrow$, shunting effectiveness $\uparrow$.

**Result:** Slow, low-frequency signals **pass through cleanly**, while fast, high-frequency signals are **doubly-stopped** — first by the inductor's resistance, then by the capacitor's short-circuit shunting to ground. This is the operational essence of every Low Pass Filter.

| Bouncer Analogy | Circuit Equivalent | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Slow-motion bouncer | Inductor $L$ | Blocks high frequencies ($X_L \propto \omega$) |
| Side-street drain | Capacitor $C$ | Shunts high frequencies to ground ($X_C \propto 1/\omega$) |
| VIP pass | Signal in pass-band | $0 \le f \le f_c$, attenuation $= 0$ |
| Banned list | Stop-band ($f > f_c$) | Signals severely attenuated |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Design Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Fundamental Design Parameters** (must be specified by the user/designer):
>
> 1. **Cutoff Frequency** $f_c$ — the boundary frequency separating pass-band from stop-band (in **Hz** or **rad/sec**).
> 2. **Design (Nominal) Impedance** $R_0$ — the characteristic impedance the filter must present at $f = 0$ (in **$\Omega$**). Mathematically, $R_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.

These two values **completely determine** the filter. Once known, $L$ and $C$ are uniquely calculated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Why and How

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Establish the K-Constraint:**
The defining property of any Constant-K filter is the impedance product relation:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_2 = (j\omega L) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = \frac{L}{C} = K^2$$

Notice how $\omega$ (frequency) **cancels out completely**. This is what makes $K$ a *constant* — it survives every frequency. This is the structural reason these filters carry the "Constant-K" label.

**Step 2 — Define the Cutoff Frequency:**
The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is where the **pass-band ends and the stop-band begins**. Mathematically, it is the frequency at which the half-section's **iterative (image) impedance** becomes purely real and matches the design resistance $R_0$. This yields:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic (Image) Impedance:**
For the two fundamental sections:

$$Z_{0T} = \sqrt{Z_1 Z_2} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}$$

$$Z_{0\pi} = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Propagation Constant & Attenuation:**
The propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ separates into:

- **Pass-band** ($f < f_c$): $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)$ — pure phase shift, zero loss.
- **Stop-band** ($f > f_c$): $\beta = 0$, $\alpha = \cosh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)$ — pure attenuation, zero phase.

**Step 5 — Why It Works in Pass-Band:**
At very low frequencies ($f \to 0$):
- $X_L = \omega L \to 0$ → Inductor is a *short-circuit* (zero opposition).
- $X_C = 1/(\omega C) \to \infty$ → Capacitor is an *open-circuit* (infinite opposition, no shunting).

So the signal passes straight through with **zero attenuation**.

**Step 6 — Why It Attenuates in Stop-Band:**
At very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$):
- $X_L = \omega L \to \infty$ → Inductor is an *open-circuit* (blocks signal).
- $X_C = 1/(\omega C) \to 0$ → Capacitor is a *short-circuit* to ground (diverts signal).

So the signal is **doubly blocked** — by the inductor at the input, and by the capacitor draining whatever gets through.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU ESE.** Every question on Constant-K LPF design reduces to a clever rearrangement of the formulas below.

| # | Formula | Description | Valid In |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K^2 = Z_1 Z_2 = L/C = R_0^2$ | Constant-K defining relation | All frequencies |
| 2 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Cutoff frequency (Hz) | Design stage |
| 3 | $\omega_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Cutoff frequency (rad/s) | Design stage |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{R_0}{\pi f_c}$ | Series inductance (Henry) | T or $\pi$ full-section |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{1}{\pi f_c R_0}$ | Shunt capacitance (Farad) | T or $\pi$ full-section |
| 6 | $Z_{0T} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}$ | Characteristic impedance, T-section | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 7 | $Z_{0\pi} = \dfrac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}}$ | Characteristic impedance, $\pi$-section | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$ | Attenuation constant (Nepers) | Stop-band ($f > f_c$) |
| 9 | $\beta = \cos^{-1}(f/f_c)$ | Phase constant (radians) | Pass-band ($f < f_c$) |
| 10 | $\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(e^\alpha) = 8.686 \alpha$ | Attenuation in dB | Stop-band |
| 11 | $Z_{1T} = L/2$, $Z_{2T} = C$ | Half T-section components | T-half |
| 12 | $Z_{1\pi} = L$, $Z_{2\pi} = 2C$ | Half $\pi$-section components | $\pi$-half |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition reminder:** At exactly $f = f_c$, the term $\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2} \to 0$, which means $Z_{0T} \to 0$ and $Z_{0\pi} \to \infty$. This is an *impedance mismatch* — the source and load see very different impedances, and reflections occur at this boundary. This is one of the key motivations for the **m-derived filter** (a higher-order extension covered later in the module).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Constant-K LPFs are the workhorse filters of analog electronics and have direct applications in:

- **Audio Engineering:** Removing high-frequency hiss/noise from audio signals (crossover networks in loudspeakers).
- **Power Electronics:** Smoothing rectified DC outputs by removing high-frequency switching ripple.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel filtering in analog telephone and early radio systems.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Anti-aliasing filters placed before analog-to-digital converters (ADCs).
- **Medical Electronics (ECG/EEG machines):** Filtering out 50/60 Hz line-noise interference and EMG high-frequency noise.

The **design procedure** is fundamentally a **two-equation, two-unknown** problem: given $f_c$ and $R_0$, solve simultaneously for $L$ and $C$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Cutoff Frequency $f_c$

**Starting point:** The image (iterative) impedance of a T-section is given by the classical image-parameter formula:

$$Z_{0T} = \sqrt{Z_1^2 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{2} + \sqrt{\frac{Z_2}{4} + Z_1 Z_2}$$

But for the simpler symmetrical network analysis, we use the **characteristic impedance** of an LC ladder:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_1 Z_2 + \frac{Z_1^2}{4}}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $Z_1 = j\omega L$ and $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{(j\omega L)\left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right) + \frac{(j\omega L)^2}{4}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the first term. Note that $(j\omega L)\left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right) = \dfrac{L}{C} = K^2$ (frequency-independent constant):

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C} - \frac{\omega^2 L^2}{4}}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\dfrac{L}{C}$ strategically:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}\left(1 - \frac{\omega^2 L C}{4}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{LC}{4}$ has units of $\dfrac{1}{\omega^2}$. Define the cutoff:

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{4}{LC} \implies \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\dfrac{L}{C} = R_0^2$. Therefore:

$$Z_0 = R_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Hz form using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}}}$$

The cutoff frequency is precisely where $\omega = \omega_c$, making $Z_0 = 0$ (purely real, zero impedance — short-circuit condition for T-section, open-circuit for $\pi$-section).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Equations for $L$ and $C$

We now have two coupled equations from the analysis above:

$$\text{(i)} \quad \omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{(ii)} \quad R_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 1:** From (ii), solve for $L$:

$$L = R_0^2 \, C$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into (i):

$$\omega_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{R_0^2 \, C \cdot C}} = \frac{2}{R_0 \, C}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $C$:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{2}{\omega_c R_0} = \frac{1}{\pi f_c R_0}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into $L = R_0^2 \, C$:

$$L = R_0^2 \cdot \frac{2}{\omega_c R_0} = \frac{2 R_0}{\omega_c}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{R_0}{\pi f_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check (dimensional analysis):**
> - $L$ has units of $\Omega / (\text{rad/s}) = \Omega \cdot \text{s} = \text{Henry}$. ✓
> - $C$ has units of $1 / (\Omega \cdot \text{rad/s}) = \text{s}/\Omega = \text{Farad}$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Attenuation in Stop-Band

**Step 1:** The propagation constant is defined as:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{Z_1}{2 Z_2} + \sqrt{\frac{Z_1^2}{4 Z_2^2} + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Z_1 = j\omega L$, $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C)$:

$$\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = (j\omega L)(j\omega C) = -\omega^2 LC$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\gamma = \cosh^{-1}\left(-\frac{\omega^2 LC}{2} + \sqrt{\frac{\omega^4 L^2 C^2}{4} - \omega^2 LC}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\omega^2 LC$ inside the square root:

$$\gamma = \cosh^{-1}\left(-\frac{\omega^2 LC}{2} + \omega\sqrt{LC}\sqrt{\frac{\omega^2 LC}{4} - 1}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Recognize $\omega^2 LC = (\omega/\omega_c)^2 \cdot 4$. After algebra, the clean result is:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j0 = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \omega > \omega_c$$

Therefore, in the **stop-band**:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right) \quad \text{Nepers}}$$

Converting to decibels (more common in practice):

$$\boxed{\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \cosh^{-1}\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right) \quad \text{dB}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Design Problem)

> **Problem:** Design a Constant-K Low Pass Filter (both T and $\pi$ sections) with cutoff frequency $f_c = 2 \text{ kHz}$ and design resistance $R_0 = 600 \text{ }\Omega$.

### Solution — Full Sections

**Step 1:** Apply the design formulas:

$$L = \frac{R_0}{\pi f_c} = \frac{600}{\pi \times 2000} = \frac{600}{6283.19} \approx 95.49 \text{ mH}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{\pi f_c R_0} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 2000 \times 600} = \frac{1}{3.77 \times 10^6} \approx 0.265 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Solution — T-Section Half

**Step 2:** Split into halves. For a **T-section**, each half contains $L/2$ in series and $C$ in shunt:

$$L_T = \frac{L}{2} \approx 47.75 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_T = C \approx 0.265 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Solution — $\pi$-Section Half

**Step 3:** For a **$\pi$-section**, each half contains $L$ in series and $2C$ in shunt:

$$L_\pi = L \approx 95.49 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_\pi = 2C \approx 0.530 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

### Verification

**Step 4:** Verify the cutoff by plugging back:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{(95.49 \times 10^{-3})(0.265 \times 10^{-6})}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{2.530 \times 10^{-8}}} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 1.591 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2000 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners give full marks only when you write **all three steps**: design formula → substitution → numerical answer with correct **units**. A bare numerical answer without units loses 1 mark.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Attenuation Calculation

> **Problem:** For the filter designed above ($f_c = 2$ kHz, $R_0 = 600 \text{ }\Omega$), find the attenuation at $f = 5 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the frequency ratio:

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{5000}{2000} = 2.5$$

**Step 2:** Since $f > f_c$, we are in the **stop-band**. Apply attenuation formula:

$$\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(2.5)$$

**Step 3:** Use identity $\cosh^{-1}(x) = \ln(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})$:

$$\alpha = \ln(2.5 + \sqrt{2.5^2 - 1}) = \ln(2.5 + \sqrt{5.25}) = \ln(2.5 + 2.291)$$

$$\alpha = \ln(4.791) \approx 1.566 \text{ Nepers}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:

$$\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \times 1.566 \approx 13.6 \text{ dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Topology — T and $\pi$ Full Sections

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T_SECTION["T SECTION FULL"]
        In1((Source)) --- L1A["L/2"]
        L1A --- NodeMidT(( ))
        NodeMidT --- L1B["L/2"]
        L1B --- Out1((Load))
        NodeMidT --- CT["C"]
        CT --- GND1((GND))
    end

    subgraph PI_SECTION["PI SECTION FULL"]
        In2((Source)) --- LP["L"]
        LP --- NodeOut2((Load))
        NodeOut2 --- Out2p(( ))
        CP1["2C"] --- NodeIn2
        NodeIn2 --- GND2((GND))
        CP2["2C"] --- NodeOut2
        Out2p --- GND3((GND))
    end
```

> **Reading the schematic:** In the T-section, the inductor $L$ is split into two halves ($L/2$ each) flanking a central shunt capacitor $C$. In the $\pi$-section, the capacitor $C$ is split into two halves ($2C$ each) flanking a central series inductor $L$.

---

## 4.2 Half-Section Prototype (The Building Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src((Vin)) --- Lh["L"]
    Lh --- Mid((Vout))
    Mid --- LoadR["R Load"]
    Mid --- Ch["C"]
    Ch --- Gnd((GND))
    style Lh fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Ch fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Half-section behavior:** This is the fundamental building block. A T-section half has $L$ in series and $C$ in shunt. Two such halves back-to-back form a full T-section. Two mirrored halves form a $\pi$-section.

---

## 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristics (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIGNAL SPECTRUM"]
        A["Signal with components at 100 Hz, 1 kHz, 10 kHz"]
    end

    subgraph FILTER["CONSTANT K LOW PASS FILTER"]
        B["Series Inductor L: blocks high f"]
        C["Shunt Capacitor C: drains high f to GND"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SPECTRUM"]
        D["100 Hz: passes unchanged"]
        E["1 kHz: passes near unchanged"]
        F["10 kHz: severely attenuated"]
    end

    A --> B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F

    style B fill:#ffd966
    style C fill:#a4c2f4
```

---

## 4.4 Attenuation vs. Frequency Curve (Block Description)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PAS["PASS BAND"]
        PB["Alpha = 0 dB, signal passes cleanly, 0 to fc"]
    end

    subgraph CUTO["CUTOFF POINT"]
        CP["f = fc, Alpha = 3 dB boundary, impedance mismatch"]
    end

    subgraph STOP["STOP BAND"]
        SB["Alpha increases monotonically as f grows beyond fc, grows logarithmically toward infinity"]
    end

    PAS --> CUTO --> STOP
    style PAS fill:#d9ead3
    style CUTO fill:#fce5cd
    style STOP fill:#f4cccc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block description of attenuation curve:** The plot has three distinct regions. (1) **Pass-band (flat plateau):** $\alpha = 0$ from $f = 0$ to $f = f_c$. (2) **Transition region:** Sharp corner at $f_c$ where attenuation begins. (3) **Stop-band (rising):** $\alpha$ grows monotonically, mathematically as $\cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$. The transition is *not infinitely sharp* — this is a known limitation of Constant-K filters and motivates m-derived designs.

---

## 4.5 Design Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Step1["Step 1: User specifies cutoff frequency fc"]
    Step2["Step 2: User specifies design resistance R0"]
    Step3["Step 3: Compute L using L = R0 divided by pi times fc"]
    Step4["Step 4: Compute C using C = 1 divided by pi times fc times R0"]
    Step5{"Step 5: Choose section type T or Pi"}
    Step6T["Step 6T: T-section uses L by 2 series, C shunt"]
    Step6P["Step 6P: Pi-section uses L series, 2C shunt"]
    Step7["Step 7: Verify cutoff using fc equals 1 over pi times square root of LC"]
    Step8["Step 8: Calculate attenuation at desired f"]

    Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6T
    Step5 --> Step6P
    Step6T --> Step7
    Step6P --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8
```

> **Reading the workflow:** The design follows a strictly linear pipeline: specification → computation → topology selection → verification → analysis. This sequence is what KTU examiners expect students to follow when answering 14-mark design problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define a Constant-K filter. Why is it called "Constant-K"?**

**Model Answer:**
A Constant-K filter is a reactive ladder network designed using the image parameter method, in which the product of the series arm impedance ($Z_1$) and the shunt arm impedance ($Z_2$) is a **constant**, real, and frequency-independent value, $K^2$. Mathematically:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_2 = K^2 = \frac{L}{C} = R_0^2$$

It is called *Constant-K* because $K$ remains the same (constant) for all operating frequencies — the $\omega$-dependence of $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ cancels out in their product. For a low-pass realization, $Z_1 = j\omega L$ (series inductor) and $Z_2 = 1/(j\omega C)$ (shunt capacitor).

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Write the expressions for characteristic impedance of T and $\pi$ sections of a Constant-K Low Pass Filter.**

**Model Answer:**
The characteristic (image) impedances for the two standard sections in the **pass-band** are:

$$Z_{0T} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}$$

$$Z_{0\pi} = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2}}$$

where $R_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ is the design resistance and $f_c = 1/(\pi\sqrt{LC})$ is the cutoff frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Choice A]**

**Q: Design a Constant-K Low Pass Filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1 \text{ kHz}$ and design resistance $R_0 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$.**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Determine the values of $L$ and $C$, and draw both T and $\pi$ full-section circuits with component values.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the characteristic impedance of the T-section at $f = 800 \text{ Hz}$, and the attenuation (in dB) at $f = 2 \text{ kHz}$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Apply the design formula for inductance:

$$L = \frac{R_0}{\pi f_c} = \frac{500}{\pi \times 1000} = \frac{500}{3141.59} \approx 159.15 \text{ mH}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the design formula for capacitance:

$$C = \frac{1}{\pi f_c R_0} = \frac{1}{\pi \times 1000 \times 500} = \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^6} \approx 0.6366 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 3:** Construct T-section:

- Series arms: $L/2 = 79.58 \text{ mH}$ each
- Shunt arm: $C = 0.6366 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$

**Step 4:** Construct $\pi$-section:

- Series arm: $L = 159.15 \text{ mH}$
- Shunt arms: $2C = 1.273 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ each

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute $f/f_c$ at $f = 800 \text{ Hz}$:

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.8$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $Z_{0T}$:

$$Z_{0T} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - (0.8)^2} = 500 \sqrt{1 - 0.64} = 500 \sqrt{0.36} = 500 \times 0.6 = 300 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3:** For attenuation at $f = 2 \text{ kHz}$, compute the frequency ratio:

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{2000}{1000} = 2.0$$

**Step 4:** Since $f > f_c$, we are in the stop-band. Apply attenuation formula:

$$\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(2) = \ln(2 + \sqrt{4-1}) = \ln(2 + \sqrt{3}) = \ln(3.732) \approx 1.317 \text{ Nepers}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to dB:

$$\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \times 1.317 \approx 11.44 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students mistakenly use $\sin^{-1}$ or $\cos^{-1}$ in the **stop-band**, which is the pass-band formula! Remember: **$\beta = \cos^{-1}(f/f_c)$ for $f < f_c$** and **$\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$ for $f > f_c$**. Wrong choice of trigonometric function costs **3 full marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice B]**

**Q: For a Constant-K Low Pass Filter, derive the expressions for cutoff frequency and design resistance. Given $L = 100 \text{ mH}$ and $C = 4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, find:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] The cutoff frequency $f_c$ and design resistance $R_0$.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] The values of components for T and $\pi$ full-sections, and the characteristic impedance at $f = 0.5 f_c$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Apply the cutoff frequency formula:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^{-6})}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the product:

$$LC = (0.1)(4 \times 10^{-6}) = 4 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.3246 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** Final cutoff frequency:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{\pi \times 6.3246 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{1.987 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 503.3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Compute design resistance:

$$R_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{25000} = 158.11 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** T-section values:

$$L_T = \frac{L}{2} = 50 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_T = C = 4 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 2:** $\pi$-section values:

$$L_\pi = L = 100 \text{ mH} \quad ; \quad C_\pi = 2C = 8 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 3:** At $f = 0.5 f_c$, the ratio $f/f_c = 0.5$:

$$Z_{0T} = R_0 \sqrt{1 - (0.5)^2} = 158.11 \sqrt{1 - 0.25} = 158.11 \sqrt{0.75} = 158.11 \times 0.866$$

$$Z_{0T} \approx 136.93 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4:** For $\pi$-section at same frequency:

$$Z_{0\pi} = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{1 - (0.5)^2}} = \frac{158.11}{0.866} \approx 182.58 \text{ }\Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A frequent mistake is writing the **shunt capacitance** for $\pi$-section as $C$ instead of $2C$. The mnemonic: *"T-section splits the L, $\pi$-section splits the C"*. Getting this wrong loses **2 marks** in design questions. Similarly, do NOT swap — T has $L/2$ (split inductor), not $2L$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Things You MUST Remember for KTU ESE — Module 4, Constant-K LPF:**

- ☐ **1.** $Z_1 Z_2 = K^2 = L/C = R_0^2$ — the defining equation of Constant-K filters.
- ☐ **2.** $f_c = 1/(\pi\sqrt{LC})$ — cutoff frequency in **Hz** (most-tested formula).
- ☐ **3.** $\omega_c = 2/\sqrt{LC}$ — cutoff frequency in **rad/s**.
- ☐ **4.** $L = R_0/(\pi f_c)$ and $C = 1/(\pi f_c R_0)$ — the two **design equations** to memorize cold.
- ☐ **5.** T-section uses $L/2$ in series, $C$ in shunt — *"split the L"*.
- ☐ **6.** $\pi$-section uses $L$ in series, $2C$ in shunt — *"split the C"*.
- ☐ **7.** $Z_{0T} = R_0\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}$ — T-section image impedance.
- ☐ **8.** $Z_{0\pi} = R_0/\sqrt{1 - (f/f_c)^2}$ — $\pi$-section image impedance.
- ☐ **9.** Pass-band ($f < f_c$): $\alpha = 0$, signal passes with only phase shift.
- ☐ **10.** Stop-band ($f > f_c$): $\alpha = \cosh^{-1}(f/f_c)$ Nepers, attenuation only.
- ☐ **11.** $\beta = \cos^{-1}(f/f_c)$ rad in pass-band — phase constant, not attenuation.
- ☐ **12.** $\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha$ — conversion from Nepers to decibels.
- ☐ **13.** At $f = 0$: $X_L = 0$ (inductor → short), $X_C = \infty$ (capacitor → open) — explains why DC/low-f passes.
- ☐ **14.** At $f \to \infty$: $X_L = \infty$ (inductor → open), $X_C = 0$ (capacitor → short to GND) — explains why high-f is blocked.
- ☐ **15.** Constant-K filters are foundational but have a sharp corner at $f_c$ (not infinitely steep roll-off) — this limitation motivates the **m-derived filter** (covered later in Module 4).

> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever you see "design" in a KTU question, immediately write down the **two design equations** ($L$ and $C$ formulas), substitute the given $R_0$ and $f_c$, and present results with proper units. This guarantees at least **10 of 14 marks**. The remaining 4 marks come from drawing the circuit correctly with $L/2$, $2C$ splits.
